protocols.title: Beginning Ritual — Quiet Arrival
protocols.duration: 2–4 minutes (at the start of every visit)
summary: Every supervised visit benefits from a small, consistent opening that gives the visit a shape. The research on ritual as container (Fiese et al. 2002) and on limited-time presence across traditions (Turner on pilgrimage; Japanese ichi-go ichi-e) converges on a single insight: when a visit has a beginning gesture, the nervous systems of everyone in the room settle into "this is the visit now." Without a beginning, the first ten minutes are spent improvising.

This ritual is also the moment where the supervisor is welcomed rather than ignored. A brief greeting reframes the supervisor from invisible authority to acknowledged presence, which changes the weather of the room.
The ritual is small on purpose. It should not require the father to remember lines, plan, or perform. It gives him a known way to enter the visit settled rather than braced.

steps.title: Acknowledge the supervisor.
steps.body: The mother (or whoever is bringing the children in) looks at the supervisor and says, simply: "Thank you for being with us today." The supervisor may or may not reply; that is their choice. This takes five seconds.

steps.title: The father settles into his seat or place in the room.
steps.body: If he is standing, the mother may invite him to sit: "Come sit with us." No pressure. If he prefers to stand, that is fine.

steps.title: One small body gesture for each child.
steps.body: The father puts one hand gently on the head, shoulder, or back of each child — whatever feels natural to him and welcome to the child. If touch is not welcome today (children sometimes shy away, and that is normal), the gesture becomes an eye-to-eye moment and a soft "hi."

steps.title: One phrase, said by the father if he can.
steps.body: Simple. It can be the same phrase every time. Examples the father may choose from: "I'm happy to see you." "Hi, my beautiful ones." "I've been waiting for today." If the father cannot find a phrase, the mother can say it for him: "Daddy's glad you're here." A phrase said for someone is still a real phrase.

steps.title: One slow breath together.
steps.body: Everyone in the room takes one slow breath. Not a "do a breathing exercise" breath. Just a quiet moment where everyone notices they are in the same room. The father, the children, the mother, the supervisor. Present.

steps.title: Transition to the planned activity.
steps.body: The mother (or whoever is leading) says: "So today we're going to [walk in the garden / read two books / play with the stones]." One sentence. Then begin.

contraindications: **Situational:** if the father arrives in acute distress, if either child is crying on arrival, if something has just happened that makes a settled opening feel false — skip the ritual and go directly to a sensory anchor (held hands, a slow breath together, sitting quietly).
**Relational:** if the supervisor has indicated this is not their first visit with the family and the opening needs to adjust, follow their lead.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: visible agitation on arrival, confusion about where he is or who the children are (different from a playful "who are these munchkins?"), tearfulness he cannot settle, pulling back from touch, flat affect replacing warmth. If any appears, the mother gently shortens the ritual to its smallest form — eye contact and one breath — and moves to a sensory anchor (water, a known song, sitting quietly). - In either child: crying on arrival, clinging to the mother, hiding behind the mother, refusing to look at the father, somatic complaints (tummy ache). A child who is not ready to greet the father is not being difficult; they are being accurate to their nervous system. Allow them their distance. The father can greet them from across the room or simply smile at them. The ritual is still honored if it is done quietly. - In yourself (the mother): suddenly tight throat, dissociation, anger rising. Take a breath. Name it to yourself. The ritual will hold while you settle. If you cannot settle, it is appropriate to ask the supervisor to give you 60 seconds to step to the side.

What to do when you see these: Shorten the ritual. Move to whatever the next activity is, slowly. Do not try to "rescue" the beginning. The visit can still be whole even if the opening was not. See 00-crisis-response-card.md if the signals are acute.
modifications: **Shorter version (60 seconds total):** Greet the supervisor. The father sits. One hand on each child's head. One slow breath together. Begin the activity.

**"Hard day" version (the father is unsettled):** Skip the phrase entirely. The mother carries the whole opening. The father just settles into his place. The body gesture is optional. The ritual becomes: "Thank you for being with us today. Let's sit." One breath. Begin.

**Wheelchair or seated version:** Exactly the same; the father's gesture is from a seated position. Some fathers may kiss the children's foreheads instead of touching heads. Any gesture that comes naturally.
traditions: This ritual draws on the universal human practice of greeting at the threshold, with specific philosophical frame from:

- Japanese tea ceremony — the principle of ichi-go ichi-e ("one time, one meeting"), which holds that every encounter is irreplaceable and deserves complete attention. Attributed to Sen no Rikyū (16th c.) and developed by Ii Naosuke. The beginning of a tea gathering is itself a ritual gesture. (Reference: Okakura, The Book of Tea, 1906.) - Jewish tradition — the optional shehecheyanu blessing as a felt-sense acknowledgment of "we are here now." (The mother may or may not draw on this; it belongs to her own relationship with the tradition.) - Quaker silent meeting — the principle that gathered silence is itself a meeting, not a failure of meeting. The "one slow breath together" draws on this. (Reference: Pink Dandelion, The Quakers: A Very Short Introduction, 2008.)
None of these traditions is privileged. The ritual is offered as a universal human form, and the mother and father may draw on whichever tradition they find meaningful, or none at all.

protocols.title: Shared Sensory Walk
summary: A slow walk outside with shared attention to small sensory things (leaves, stones, birds, water) is one of the most research-supported practices at the intersection of "works for a cognitively-changing parent," "works for young children," and "works with supervision present." Forest-bathing research (Li 2010; Park et al. 2010) shows lower cortisol and settled nervous systems after time in natural settings. Nature-based interventions for cognitive change (Clark et al.; Rappe 2005) show reduced agitation. Children's nature research (Chawla 2015; Louv 2005) shows emotional regulation and attentional recovery.
The walk is shared-attention, not shared-conversation. No one has to plan, remember, or perform. The father walks alongside his children and notices the world with them. That is the whole practice.

steps.title: Begin with the beginning ritual
steps.body: (`01-beginning-ritual-quiet-arrival.md`) — the walk is the "today we're going to walk" part of the transition.

steps.title: Walk slowly.
steps.body: Not "children at play" pace, and not "adults exercising" pace. The pace of a 5-year-old noticing a beetle. This pace is often the natural pace of a father whose cognition is shifting; it may be the first time in a while that the family's natural pace matches his.

steps.title: Set a simple shared task.
steps.body:
One of:
   - *Leaf-finding*: "Let's see how many different kinds of leaves we can find." The children gather. The father points out ones he sees. Keep them in the bag or basket.
   - *Stone-sorting*: "Let's find five smooth stones." Same principle. The children can give the best one to the father, who holds it.
   - *Bird-listening*: "Let's see how many different bird sounds we can hear." Everyone stops and listens when someone hears one. No scorekeeping.
   - *Color-finding*: "Let's find something blue. Now something yellow." The children run and find; the father nods and smiles at what they bring back.

steps.title: The father's role is to be present, not to lead.
steps.body: He can comment, point, nod, smile, hold what the children bring him. He does not have to know the names of things, remember names from last time, or produce content. The children lead the finding; he witnesses and appreciates.

steps.title: Pause for water if anyone gets thirsty.
steps.body: The pause is itself part of the walk.

steps.title: End when the children's attention starts to scatter, or when you reach a natural stopping point
steps.body: (a bench, the far side of the path, the end of a set time). Do not push through the "bored" phase. Short and settled is better than long and stretched.

steps.title: Walk back to the visit space
steps.body: with what you gathered. The gathering becomes a small artifact the children can hold or show.

steps.title: Transition to the closing ritual
steps.body: (`04-closing-ritual-three-glads.md`) when you return, or to another activity if the visit has more time.

contraindications: **Absolute:** if the father is unsteady on his feet today (fall risk) — **operationalized fall-risk check before each walk:** observe his gait as he stands from the chair (shuffling, toe-drag, wide-based instability, needing a hand to stand). Has he fallen in the last 2 weeks? Has any new medication been started that may cause orthostasis (dizziness on standing)? If any of these apply, do the seated version (see Modifications) instead. **Check footwear:** non-slip shoes, no untied laces, no flip-flops. **Avoid uneven terrain:** tree-root heave, loose gravel, stairs, curbs, wet or icy surfaces. A paved, flat path is always safer. **In hot weather:** shorten the walk, stay in shade, offer water before starting and at the midpoint; if the father shows confusion or fatigue in heat, stop and move indoors (dehydration and heat can trigger confusion rapidly in cognitive change). If the children are in acute distress that outdoor walking would not help; if the weather makes it unsafe.

**Relative:** crowded parks where the ambient noise and distraction would be overstimulating; unfamiliar locations where the father may become disoriented (prefer the same place every time for the first several visits).

**Caution:** if either child is preoccupied with something emotional from the trip over (a hard conversation, a separation from the mother if she is not coming on the walk), address that before starting the walk, not during it.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: unsteadiness, fatigue (slowing beyond the walk's natural pace, asking to sit), confusion about where he is, repetitive questions, growing agitation or frustration. If any appear: slow down, offer his water, sit on the nearest bench, let the children play nearby for a moment. If the father is not settling, turn back toward the visit space. - In either child: clinginess to the mother, boredom that becomes whining, concerns about the father ("Is Daddy tired?" "Why is Daddy walking slow?"), physical complaints (tummy ache, tired legs). If any appear: the walk has reached its natural end. Turn back. A 10-minute walk that ended with everyone present is a successful walk. A 30-minute walk that ended with one child whining is a walk that went too long. - In yourself (the mother): sudden emotional surge (the sight of your family walking together may trigger grief), dissociation from the moment, impatience. If any appear: take one slow breath. You do not have to manage your feelings perfectly to lead the walk. Acknowledge the surge silently and keep walking. If the surge is too large to hold, ask the supervisor if they can stand with the father and children for a moment while you step aside.
What to do when you see these signals: See 00-crisis-response-card.md if any crosses into acute territory. Otherwise: slow down, shorten, return.
modifications: **Shorter version (10 minutes):** A walk around a single small loop in the nearest green space. The father and children pick up one or two small things. Return.

**Lower-energy version (seated):** If the father cannot walk today, sit together on a bench somewhere outdoors and *notice* instead of walking. "What do you hear? What color is that flower? Where is the bird?" The practice is still shared attention, just without locomotion.

**"Hard day" version:** If the outdoor space is too overwhelming today, do the practice indoors near a window. Look at what's outside. Notice the weather, the sky, the one tree you can see. The sensory orientation is the practice; the outdoors is the easier venue, not a requirement.
**Wheelchair-accessible version:** Choose paths that are paved and not too hilly; the father's pace is now your pace. Many parks have accessible loops specifically designed for this.
traditions: - Japanese Shinrin-yoku (forest bathing) — the research tradition originating in Japan that established the physiological benefits of time in forested environments. (Reference: Li 2010; Park et al. 2010.) - Indigenous philosophical orientation — Mitákuye Oyás'iŋ ("all my relations") as a frame for kinship with the more-than-human world. Reference: Kimmerer, Braiding Sweetgrass (2013), as an openly published Potawatomi source. Note: specific Lakota ceremonial practices around land and relations are not drawn on here; this is philosophical orientation only. - Zen walking meditation (kinhin) — the practice of walking with awareness, one step at a time, without a destination. Referenced philosophically; not taught as a practice to be learned.
No single tradition is privileged. The walk is a universal human activity. The traditions are named because they each offer a window onto why walking together with attention matters.

protocols.title: Picture Book Together
summary: Picture books are designed for shared attention across any gap — between a toddler and an adult, between two people who speak different languages, between a child and a parent whose cognition is shifting. Even if the father cannot remember the plot later, he can read the words on the page in front of him, and the shared reading is real. One of the quiet reversals this practice allows: the children can read TO the father, which preserves his dignity (he is not being tested) while honoring what they can now do.

Phase A synthesis §3.3 notes that procedural and musical memory often outlast narrative memory. Reading aloud is a procedural act: eyes track words, voice forms sounds, body sits close to another body. None of this requires remembering yesterday's book.

steps.title: Sit close together.
steps.body: The father in the middle if possible, one child on each side. Or the father on one end, both children next to him. Body contact is part of the practice.

steps.title: Hand the book to the father.
steps.body: Let him hold it. He reads if he can. If reading is hard today, the 8yo reads aloud while the father and 5yo listen. If no one wants to read, the three of them look at the pictures and the father points to things ("Look, a moon. Look, a little mouse.").

steps.title: Pause on pages the children love.
steps.body: Let them talk about the picture. Let the father respond however he does — a smile, "yes," a pointing finger, a laugh. He does not have to produce content; he produces presence.

steps.title: One or two books per practice, not more.
steps.body: Long reading sessions get tiring. Stop before anyone is bored.

steps.title: End with the book closed on the father's lap for a moment.
steps.body: A small pause before moving to the next activity.

contraindications: **Absolute:** books with themes of loss, illness, or dying parents — even sweetly framed ones — unless a therapist has specifically recommended them. This is not the moment for a processing-the-situation book. This is a together-being book.
**Relative:** books the father chose in the past but now has difficulty reading (long sentences, small text, complex plots). Pre-screen for accessibility.

**Caution:** books that were recently read to the children by someone else and may cause a child to correct the father's reading. If that happens, gently redirect — "Daddy's reading now, we can hear it again."
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: struggling with a word repeatedly, frustration, asking "what does this say?" multiple times. If he is struggling, the 8yo takes over reading without fanfare. - In the 5yo: losing interest, wriggling, wanting to leave. Stop reading; move to another activity. A 5-minute read is successful. - In the 8yo: correcting the father, becoming parental. Gently: "Let's just hear Daddy read." If she cannot let go of the correction impulse, she can hold the book and read while the father and 5yo listen. - In yourself: tears surfacing as you watch your family read together. Allow them quietly. Do not interrupt the practice to manage them.
modifications: **Shorter (5 minutes):** one short book, or half of one book.
**"Hard day":** the father doesn't read. He holds the book. The mother or the 8yo reads. He listens, looks, smiles.
**If there are no picture books at the visit location:** a children's magazine, a seed catalog, an illustrated field guide, anything with pictures and simple words.
traditions: - The universal human practice of reading aloud together. - Christian lectio divina — slow contemplative reading — referenced philosophically, not as a practice being taught. The principle that reading slowly and out loud with people you love is itself sacred. (Reference: Guigo II's four-stage model, 12th c., as the traditional framework.) - Japanese monogatari — story-as-gift — a cultural frame in which the story told to a child is a form of legacy. (Noted as philosophical framing; not extraction.)
Picture books are open to every family and every tradition. Use what your children love.

protocols.title: Closing Ritual — Three Glads
protocols.duration: 2–5 minutes (at the end of every visit)
summary: A visit with a clear ending leaves everyone with a shape to hold afterward. The research on visit-bracketing (Phase A synthesis §3.5) converges with both attachment research and ritual theory: children need to know a visit is ending before it ends, and a small closing gesture helps the transition be graceful rather than abrupt. Without a closing, the end feels like a rupture.

"Three glads" is a small shared moment in which each person (including the father, if he can, and including the children) names one thing they are glad about from today. Not "what was your favorite part" — that is a graded question. Just one gladness. Small is fine. Quiet is fine. "I am glad we walked" is enough. Silence is also allowed.

steps.title: Announce the ending.
steps.body: 5 minutes before end: "We have a little bit more time, then we'll do our glads, then it's time to go."

steps.title: Gather.
steps.body: Everyone sits close together, the same way they did for the beginning ritual.

steps.title: One gladness each.
steps.body: Go in order: mother first, then 5yo, then 8yo, then father. (The mother goes first to model tone — matter-of-fact, small, specific. "I'm glad we walked in the garden today." Not performative. Not poetic.) Each person gets the same small space. If someone cannot name a gladness, they can say "pass" or "I don't have one today," and that is honored.

steps.title: The father last.
steps.body: If he can name one, he does. If he says "pass," the mother gently says, "Daddy's glad you were here," on his behalf. A gladness said for someone is still real.

steps.title: One hug, one goodbye, one small phrase.
steps.body: The mother's choice: "See you next time, Daddy." The father's response, whatever he can offer. The children's goodbyes.

steps.title: Move toward leaving without rushing.
steps.body: Gathering coats, bag, water bottles. The transition is part of the ritual.

contraindications: **Situational:** if the visit ended early due to distress, skip the full ritual and do a 30-second version (one shared breath, a hug, "see you next time").
**Relational:** if either child is in a state where saying "glad" feels false, do not force it. The ritual can be: "Three things we noticed today" or "Three things we did" instead.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: difficulty finding words at all, confusion about what is being asked, tearfulness at the ending. If any appear: shorten to one hand squeeze and "see you next time." The ritual is honored by being done at all, not by being complete. - In either child: sadness as the end approaches (expected and appropriate; do not try to fix it), clinging ("can we stay longer?"), tantrum about leaving. If any: honor the feeling without extending the visit. "I know it's hard. We'll see Daddy again on [day]." Hold the leaving. - In yourself: overwhelming sadness at the goodbye. Feel it; do not perform around it. The children will see that goodbyes can be sad and survived.
modifications: **Shorter (60 seconds):** No glads. Just: everyone stands, one shared look, one hug each, "See you next time." Done.
**If a child cannot find a gladness:** "Three things we did" instead. The children name one concrete thing each. The father tries one. The mother covers any gaps.

**If the father is very confused at closing:** the mother handles the whole ritual verbally on everyone's behalf. The father just receives it. This is not a failure; this is the ritual adapting to what is possible today.
traditions: - Ignatian Examen — Christian (Jesuit) practice of daily review noticing consolations (moments of given-life). Cited philosophically as the frame for noticing small gladnesses. (Reference: Fleming 2008.) - Universal human practice — across cultures, endings of gatherings are marked with a small shared moment. The Tibetan dedication of merit at the end of practice, the Christian "peace be with you" at the end of a Mass, the Jewish "shalom" at parting — all are examples of marking that a gathering is ending and something has been held.
The ritual is offered as a universal form, shaped to this family's specific context.

protocols.title: Sensory Bin Play
summary: A sensory bin is a container (a shoebox lid, a small tray, a low tub) filled with a simple tactile material — dry rice, dried beans, smooth stones, kinetic sand, water beads — with small scoops, cups, and open-ended toys for stirring, pouring, and sorting. It is a staple of early-childhood education because it provides rich sensory engagement with no right answer, no time limit, and no required expertise. For a father whose cognition is changing, the sensory bin is a generous ally: he can be alongside the children in play without needing to lead, plan, or remember.

Phase A synthesis §3.3 discusses the Snoezelen principle — low-demand, high-sensory, shared presence — adapted to dementia care. The sensory bin is the home version. (Note: the research on Snoezelen in dementia contexts is mixed at the outcome level but consistent that such environments reduce agitation during sessions. See Chung & Lai 2009 Cochrane review for cautious synthesis.)

steps.title: Set up the bin on a low table or on the floor on a mat.
steps.body: Enough space for three people to reach into it from different sides.

steps.title: Introduce it simply.
steps.body: "Here is some rice and some cups. You can pour and stir and dig. Daddy's going to play with us." No instructions beyond that.

steps.title: Everyone reaches in.
steps.body: The father and children play in parallel — pouring, stirring, scooping, burying a small toy and finding it again. There is no goal.

steps.title: Follow the children's lead.
steps.body: If the 5yo starts a game of "hide the stone and find it," the father plays along. If the 8yo sorts by color, the father can sort with her.

steps.title: Let there be quiet stretches.
steps.body: The practice does not need constant conversation. A minute of quiet hands in rice is a real minute of being together.

steps.title: End when attention scatters.
steps.body: Do not push through "I'm bored." Five minutes of settled play is better than 20 minutes of stretched play.

steps.title: Simple cleanup together.
steps.body: Everyone helps put scoops back and pat down any spilled rice. The cleanup is part of the practice, not an afterthought.

contraindications: **Absolute:** any filler that is a choking hazard for the 5yo — nothing smaller than the width of a toilet-paper tube (~3.2 cm / small-parts choke test cylinder standard). **Water beads are Absolute: do not use** — they are a known serious pediatric ingestion hazard and have been subject to product-safety recalls; they expand in the digestive tract and can cause life-threatening bowel obstruction. Small beads, small buttons, coins, marbles, and anything a 5yo could swallow are prohibited. **The 5yo is never alone with the bin; an adult must be within arm's reach at all times.** Any hand-to-mouth behavior by the 5yo is a full-stop signal — redirect immediately and consider whether this is the right practice for this child today
**Relative:** fillers that scatter widely and require intensive cleanup (water beads, small foam bits) in visit locations where cleanup would be a burden

**Caution:** if the father has any tendency to put small objects in his mouth (rare but relevant in some progressive conditions); if a child has texture sensitivities that make a specific filler distressing
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: losing interest quickly, confusion about what is being done, frustration with a small task (unable to fit the scoop in the cup), spilling more than is comfortable. If any appear: shift to simpler motions — just hands in the rice, no scoops. Or move to a different practice (songs, picture book, walk). - In the 5yo: boredom after a few minutes (totally normal — 5 minutes is a full practice for this age in this format), tossing rice out of the bin (needs redirection), eating rice (needs immediate redirection). If any: redirect gently or end the practice. - In the 8yo: finding the bin "babyish" and opting out. Offer her the 8-year-old-interest version (color sorting, counting, small-object finding) or let her watch while reading her own book nearby. She does not have to participate if it does not fit her today. - In yourself: tears watching the father play with his children in a way that may look regressed from an outside view. Allow them. The father is not regressed; the practice is age-inclusive. The rice does not judge.
modifications: **Shorter (5 minutes):** One cup, one scoop, a handful of rice, five minutes. Done.
**Lower-energy:** Just two people (father and 5yo while 8yo reads or draws nearby). Less coordination required.
**"Hard day":** Skip the bin; put a bowl of smooth stones on the table and let everyone hold them. Holding smooth stones together is the minimum viable sensory practice.
**If the location cannot accommodate a sensory bin:** a small bag of dried beans or stones everyone can reach into is a portable version.
traditions: - Early childhood education tradition — the sensory bin is a staple of Montessori-influenced and Reggio Emilia-influenced preschool practice, grounded in the recognition that tactile exploration is how young children regulate and learn. - Snoezelen multisensory environment tradition — developed by Ad Verheul and Jan Hulsegge in the Netherlands in the 1970s, originally for people with intellectual disability and later adapted for dementia care. (Reference: Hulsegge & Verheul 1987; Chung & Lai 2009 for cautious synthesis of dementia-context evidence.)
This is a secular, open, universal practice. No lineage attribution beyond the traditions above.

protocols.title: Songs We Remember
summary: Of all practices in this kit, singing together may be the one most directly grounded in the neuroscience of presence-without-memory. Oliver Sacks, in Musicophilia (2007), documented that musical memory is neurologically distinct from narrative memory and often persists remarkably long into progressive cognitive change. The case of Clive Wearing — the musician with profound amnesia whose conducting and piano-playing remained intact — became iconic because it showed how deeply music is held in procedural and emotional memory systems that declarative memory loss does not reach.

The implication for a young father who once sang lullabies to his children as babies: those songs may still be available to him when their names sometimes are not. Humming and singing also have their own somatic effects — long exhales, vagal stimulation, gentle chest and throat vibration — that support co-regulation for everyone in the room (Phase A synthesis §3.3 on polyvagal theory).

steps.title: The mother starts a song.
steps.body: Not "let's sing" as an invitation. Just: the mother begins humming or singing, softly. "Twinkle twinkle little star..." or whatever comes first from the list.

steps.title: Wait and see if the father joins.
steps.body: Procedural music memory often activates a beat or two into a familiar song. His voice may come in automatically. Or his body may start moving — gentle rocking, tapping. Or his face may soften. All of these count as joining.

steps.title: The children join next.
steps.body: At 5 and 8, singing with parents is developmentally welcome. Sing at kid-friendly volume; do not turn it into a performance.

steps.title: Do not stop if the father loses the words.
steps.body: He can hum. Humming is still singing. Everyone humming together is still singing together.

steps.title: 2–4 songs per practice, not more.
steps.body: Short songs, repeated if everyone wants, are better than long songs once.

steps.title: End softly.
steps.body: After the last song, let there be a moment of quiet before transitioning. The song continues resonating for a few seconds after the voices stop.

contraindications: **Relative:** if the father is in a withdrawn or flat-affect state today, singing may feel false. Offer but do not force.

**Caution:** songs that may surface grief unexpectedly. Some lullabies carry deep emotional weight for parents, especially lullabies sung to children who are now older. Watch for tears and honor them.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: sudden silence mid-song (fine — let it pass without commenting), tears (acknowledge quietly: "yeah, this one," and keep going if he wants or stop if he wants), confusion about where he is or what is being sung, inability to follow the melody. If any: drop to humming. Humming is the floor of this practice; if he can hum, the practice is still complete. - In either child: wriggling restlessness (may just want a different song — try another one); a child becoming suddenly quiet or sad if a song carries a memory (honor it; ask gently "you okay?"); a child who simply doesn't want to sing today (let them listen). - In yourself: tears, especially at lullabies sung to the children as babies. Allow them. Sing through them if you can. If you cannot, let the 8yo or the father or the humming carry the song while you settle.
modifications: **Shorter (5 minutes):** one song, maybe humming for a minute afterward.
**"Hard day":** no singing; just humming together. Or play a recorded version of a song the father loves and everyone listens to it.
**If the father is not ready to sing today:** the mother and children sing softly while the father listens. He is not excluded; he is receiving the song. That is also a practice.
**For the 8yo who finds group singing babyish:** she can listen, or pick a song she likes, or hum along rather than singing.
traditions: - Universal human singing tradition — every known culture sings to children. Lullabies are one of the oldest and most widespread forms of human music-making. - Jewish nigun — wordless melody as container of meaning, particularly Hasidic. Referenced philosophically only. - Oliver Sacks's clinical observations on music and memory — named as a scientific framework supporting why this practice matters for a family in this situation. (Reference: Sacks, Musicophilia, 2007.) - Celtic and Gaelic wake-singing traditions (caoineadh and related) — referenced as a historical example of voice carrying what words alone could not. Not being taught here as a practice; referenced philosophically. [Note: this is the only Celtic reference in this protocol and is framed as historical-example-only, per the Ethics Guardian review's concern about caoineadh attribution.]

protocols.title: Simple Snack Together
summary: Sharing food is the most universal human gesture of being together. Every culture does it; every family knows how. For a supervised visit, a simple snack shared and assembled together offers: shared activity (cutting, arranging, pouring), shared eating, low cognitive demand, and the tactile-sensory engagement Phase A synthesis §3.3 identifies as load-bearing for presence-without-memory. The father does not have to plan or remember. He can cut fruit next to his children, hand them crackers, eat an apple slice. These motions are procedural.

The practice also gently inverts the supervised-visit power dynamic: instead of the father being observed, he is providing for or sharing with his children — offering the apple slice, passing the cup. That small reversal, small as it is, is dignity-preserving.

steps.title: Bring the snack materials out onto the table.
steps.body: Simple containers, no ceremony.

steps.title: Invite participation.
steps.body: "Let's make our snack together." The children help arrange crackers on plates, put apple slices on a tray, pour juice (with adult help for the 5yo).

steps.title: The father's role: one small concrete task
steps.body: he can do today. Examples: arranging crackers on his own plate, handing a napkin to each child, pouring water into his own cup, peeling a tangerine. Choose the task that fits his current capacity. Do not ask him "what do you want to help with?" — that is a recall question. Instead, hand him something: "Daddy, will you pass these crackers to [child]?" A direct small task.

steps.title: Sit and eat together.
steps.body: The conversation, if any, is about the snack ("this apple is crunchy") or about the day ("we saw a squirrel on the walk"). No deep topics.

steps.title: The 8yo may enjoy a slightly more grown-up task
steps.body: : opening a juice bottle carefully, cutting soft cheese with a child-safe butter knife under supervision.

steps.title: Clean up together.
steps.body: Putting wrappers in the trash, folding napkins, wiping the table with a damp paper towel. The cleanup is part of the practice; it extends the shared time and gives the father another concrete task.

contraindications: **Absolute:** any food the father has difficulty swallowing due to condition-related dysphagia — **dysphagia signals to watch for:** coughing during or after eating/drinking, wet or gurgly voice after swallowing, repeated throat-clearing during meals, food pocketing in the cheek, prolonged chewing without swallowing, avoidance of particular textures, recent weight loss. **If any of these appear, stop the snack practice entirely and contact the care team (speech-language pathologist or neurologist) before reintroducing food activities.** Pre-screen snack textures with the care team before the first snack practice. Prefer soft, cohesive, moist foods (banana slices, ripe pear, soft cheese, yogurt) over dry, crumbly foods (crackers, pretzels) on the first practice and until the care team clears other textures. Follow any care-team guidance about thickened liquids — if no guidance exists and the father coughs on thin liquids (water, juice), stop and consult. **Father must be seated upright with feet flat; do not offer food if he is reclined, drowsy, or has just woken.** If the father coughs significantly during or after eating, contact the care team same day; if the cough is severe or he has difficulty breathing, call emergency services (aspiration pneumonia is a delayed risk). Any nut allergens; anything the 5yo could choke on (grapes must be cut lengthwise into quarters per current AAP guidance for children under 5)

**Relative:** messy foods in locations where the supervisor needs the space kept tidy; cold/hot items that require careful temperature management; anything the father used to prepare skillfully and may feel loss about
**Caution:** nostalgic foods ("Daddy's famous cookies") that may spotlight what he can no longer make. Prefer neutral foods without memory weight.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: difficulty with a task that used to be easy (opening a jar, pouring), visible frustration, chewing or swallowing difficulty, confusion about what he is doing. If any: gently take over the task without comment. "Let me get that for you." Do not draw attention to the difficulty. - In either child: not hungry (fine — they can participate without eating; they can put crackers on the plate and then sit), refusing to eat with the father (rare but possible; honor it if it comes up — they can eat separately while near him). Spilling or making a mess (normal; clean up together without fuss). - In yourself: sudden tears watching your family eat together in the supervised visit space. Allow them quietly. Sip water. Keep the practice gentle.
modifications: **Shorter (5 minutes):** A single shared item — a handful of crackers, one apple sliced, one cup of water each. Sit for 3 minutes eating quietly. Done.
**"Hard day" (father is tired):** No assembly; the mother brings pre-plated snacks. The father simply eats next to the children. Sharing space is the practice.
**If the visit location forbids food:** a cup of water each is still a shared practice. Drinking water together can be a complete gesture.
traditions: - Universal human practice of shared food — the most widespread form of being-together across cultures - Jewish seudah — the shared meal as inherently sacred, even without formal blessing. Reference: this is a mainstream Jewish concept, discussed in standard sources like Lamm's The Jewish Way (2000). - Christian hospitality tradition — from the Benedictine Rule Chapter 53 ("Let all guests be received as Christ") to the Eucharist as a sharing-of-meal-made-sacred. Philosophical reference only. - Islamic iftar tradition — breaking fast with community as an act of care. Philosophical reference only; not being taught as a practice.
All traditions converge on a simple truth: food offered with love between people who love each other is its own form of prayer, no matter what anyone calls it.

protocols.title: Shared Drawing — Each Adds
summary: This is a deepening-level practice because it produces an artifact the children can keep, and that low-pressure artifact-creation requires the family to have enough settled rhythm in earlier practices to hold this together. A single large sheet of paper on which everyone adds something — a line, a color, a shape, a small drawing — is collaborative without requiring any single person to produce something meaningful on their own. The father does not have to remember how to draw a specific thing. He can add a squiggle. He can color in a shape a child has started. He can press his palm to the paper and trace his own hand. All of these are contributions.

Phase A synthesis §3.5 on creative engagement in dementia care (Basting, 2009, Forget Memory) supports this approach: the measure is presence, not memory; the output is the shared attention, not the aesthetic result.

steps.title: Lay the paper flat.
steps.body: Put the art materials in small bowls or containers so everyone can reach them easily.

steps.title: Introduce it simply.
steps.body: "We're going to draw on this paper together. Everyone can add whatever they want. There's no right way to do this."

steps.title: Start the father with a simple entry.
steps.body: Hand him a crayon. Suggest (gently, without pressure) "Daddy, you can make the first mark. Or we can make it together." If he hesitates, the mother traces a small line and hands him the crayon.

steps.title: Everyone adds in parallel.
steps.body: The 5yo scribbles, colors, makes shapes. The 8yo may draw recognizable things (flowers, a sun, a stick figure). The father adds — whatever he can, however he can. A line. A color fill. A shape. A handprint (he presses his palm flat, the mother traces around it).

steps.title: Let the drawing evolve.
steps.body: If a child starts drawing a tree, the father can add leaves. If he adds a circle, a child can add a smile inside it. The drawing is a dialogue in marks.

steps.title: Pauses are fine.
steps.body: Quiet work is fine. Conversation is fine ("I like your blue"). Do not require narration.

steps.title: End at a natural stopping point
steps.body: — when the paper feels full, or when the children's attention shifts, or when the practice time is up. Do not over-fill the paper or push past the natural ending.

steps.title: The drawing becomes an artifact.
steps.body: Decide together what happens to it: stays at the visit location for next time, or the mother takes it home, or one child takes it home. Let the children help decide.

contraindications: **Relative:** if the father has a history of being a skilled artist and now struggles to draw at his former level — this practice may surface frustration. Pre-discuss with the care team or family therapist whether it serves him.

**Caution:** if either child has started using drawing as a high-stakes performance space (art class anxiety, perfectionism), this may import that stress. If so, use it only if the child enters voluntarily.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: visible frustration ("I used to be able to draw this"), embarrassment, hesitation to add anything. If any: simplify to handprint tracing or color-filling. "Daddy, just color this corner blue for me." A direct concrete task he can succeed at. - In either child: perfectionism surfacing ("I messed up!"), wanting to erase the father's marks, hogging the paper. If any: gently remind that the drawing is collaborative. "There's no mess up on this paper. Let's all keep adding." - In yourself: grief at watching what your family was, compared with what it is. Allow it quietly. The drawing is still being made.
modifications: **Shorter (10 minutes):** a smaller sheet, fewer materials, one contribution each.
**"Hard day":** the father presses his palm flat and the mother traces around it — that's his contribution. The children draw around his handprint. Minimum viable practice.
**If the location cannot accommodate a large paper:** individual small drawings that the family trades (each person draws one thing, hands it to the next, who adds to it). Round-robin version.
**For the 8yo:** if she wants more structure, she can lead a theme ("let's all draw things that live in a garden") — the father contributes what he can within the theme.
traditions: - Universal human practice of collaborative art-making — from cave paintings to contemporary family art projects - Japanese haiga — brief brush drawings with poetry — referenced philosophically as "small marks matter." (Reference: see the tradition of Matsuo Bashō's haiga as brief shared works.) - Art therapy research in dementia care — Anne Basting's work, including the TimeSlips creative engagement program, specifically establishes that creative practice without a "right answer" is both protective and generative for people with cognitive change. (Reference: Basting, Forget Memory, 2009.)
The practice is offered as a universal form. Nothing about it requires drawing skill or art-world knowledge. The marks are real because they were made together.

protocols.title: Water Listening
summary: As the father's condition progresses, practices shift toward simpler, not more complex. Water listening is one of the simplest practices in the kit — sitting together near flowing water (a fountain, a stream, a pond with a circulating pump, even a garden faucet running into a basin) and listening. Minimal conversation. No plan. Just the water and the family.

Phase A synthesis §3.3 names water as a sensory anchor across traditions, and §3.5 specifically mentions water activities as suitable for the intersection of cognitive limitation, young children's attention, and supervision. The sound of flowing water is a nonverbal auditory anchor — the nervous system recognizes it without requiring interpretation.

This is a deepening-level practice because it asks the family to be content with less stimulation, less activity, less narrative. Earlier-stage practices (walks, books, singing) are more active. This one is quieter. It is the practice a family may move toward as the father's condition shifts and as all three generations become more comfortable with simple shared presence.

steps.title: Sit near the water.
steps.body: Close enough to hear it clearly, far enough to be safe. Everyone sits — the father, the children, the mother, the supervisor if they wish. (The supervisor often appreciates being allowed to simply sit quietly during this practice.)

steps.title: Say only this:
steps.body: "Let's just listen for a minute."

steps.title: Listen.
steps.body: One minute of shared listening. Minimum. Longer is welcome if everyone is settled.

steps.title: Optional: notice one thing.
steps.body: After the listening minute, anyone can say one thing they noticed. "I heard the water splashing." "I heard a bird." "I heard Daddy breathing." Not everyone has to. Quiet is a legitimate noticing.

steps.title: Optional: one finger in the water.
steps.body: If safe, one hand lightly dipped, one fingertip in the water, one moment of cool. The father can do this, the children can do this, the mother can do this. The sensation is direct.

steps.title: Longer sits are welcome but not required.
steps.body: Some visits this practice is 5 minutes; some days it is 20. Let the children's attention set the length.

steps.title: End when the mother feels the moment is complete
steps.body: — not when the clock says the practice is over. Move to the next activity or to the closing ritual.

contraindications: **Absolute:** any water source where a child could fall in — specifically: **no water deeper than ankle-depth within the 5yo's reach unless there is a physical barrier between child and water**. Drowning can occur in less than 2 inches (5 cm) of water. "Flowing water" for this practice means a contained fountain, a tabletop fountain, a faucet running into a basin, or a controlled stream feature with hard edges. **Natural streams, ponds without barriers, pool edges, and any open body of water where a child could fall in are Absolute: do not use.** The **safest version is a faucet running into a basin indoors** — use this as the default; outdoor natural water is the advanced option with explicit safety setup. **Touch supervision required:** one designated adult per child, eyes on the child, no phones, no attention-drifting conversation. This is especially important because the practice is quiet and meditative — adults may drift into their own listening, which is exactly the condition in which drownings happen. **Dry, non-slippery footing within 1 meter of any water source** — no wet stones, moss-covered edges, or slippery surfaces. Father seated with stable back support within 1 meter of any water source; never standing on wet edges. **If any child enters the water unexpectedly:** retrieve immediately, assess for aspiration (coughing, sputtering, confusion), contact pediatrician same day even if child seems fine (delayed aspiration/secondary drowning is rare but real); see `00-crisis-response-card.md`
**Relative:** cold weather making outdoor water unpleasant or unsafe; any water source the supervisor has not cleared
**Caution:** if water has specific grief associations for the family (a memory of something connected to water), prefer a neutral water source
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: restlessness after a few minutes (move to a different activity), sudden tears at the sound of water (can be beautiful; ask gently "you okay?"; honor the tears if they want to come; do not try to fix), dissociation or blank affect. - In either child: boredom (totally normal — 5 minutes is often a maximum for a 5yo with no other stimulation), wanting to throw things in the water (redirect: "we're just listening today"), fear of water (honor it completely; move away from water). - In yourself: grief surfacing with the water sound. Allow it. Water is often a grief-carrier across cultures for exactly this reason.
modifications: **Shorter (2 minutes):** sit, listen, one shared breath, move on. A 2-minute water practice is real.
**Indoors / no water source:** play a recording of flowing water at low volume in a quiet room. The auditory anchor is the practice; the live water is better but not required.
**If the children cannot sit still:** let them move gently near the water (walking a path beside it, touching it safely) while still listening as a group.

**As the father's condition progresses:** this practice can extend longer and become a higher percentage of the visit. The deepening version of the whole configuration is visits that look more like this and less like the active entry-level practices.
traditions: Water is sacred across nearly every tradition. The practice draws on this universal truth without claiming any specific tradition:

- Christian baptism and holy water — philosophical reference; not a baptismal practice - Jewish mikvah (ritual immersion) — referenced only as philosophical acknowledgment that water is held as sacred in Jewish tradition; the mikvah itself is a specific lineage practice - Islamic wudu — ritual washing before prayer, as a mainstream Muslim practice; referenced philosophically - Kneipp water therapy — European Catholic/secular tradition of water application for health and regulation, associated with Sebastian Kneipp (19th c.) - Hindu reverence for flowing water — the Ganges and other sacred rivers; philosophical reference only - Japanese Shinto purification — the misogi tradition referenced philosophically - Zen garden tradition — water features in Zen gardens are designed for exactly this kind of attentive listening; philosophical reference

Indigenous water traditions are acknowledged but not drawn on as practices — many Indigenous communities hold specific water ceremonies that are closed to outsiders. The universal human reverence for water is the frame here, not any specific ceremony.
The practice of listening to water together is the family's own form. It joins an ancient human conversation without claiming any specific inheritance.

protocols.title: Morning Anchor
summary: A single shared moment at the start of the day gives everyone a shape to hang the day on. The research on family rituals as protective factors (Fiese et al. 2002) and the attachment literature's emphasis on predictable routine (Bowlby 1969/1982) converge: children under stress do better when the day begins the same way every time. The mother under stress does better too — a 3-minute anchor is not a burden; it is a frame that keeps the day from starting in freefall.
This is not a meditation, not a prayer, not a therapy session. It is a brief shared moment of "here we are, this is today."

steps.title: Stand or sit together in the kitchen or living room.
steps.body: No special arrangement. Wherever the family naturally gathers in the morning.

steps.title: The mother says one sentence.
steps.body:
Choose one that fits and keep it the same every day:
   - "Good morning, family. Here we are."
   - "Thank you for this day."
   - "We are together right now."
   - Or a tradition-specific opening if the family has one (a short blessing, a prayer, a named intention — see Traditions honored below).

steps.title: One shared breath.
steps.body: Everyone takes one slow breath together. Not coached breathing — just a collective moment of "we are in the same room, breathing the same air."

steps.title: Name the plan for today.
steps.body: The mother says, in one or two sentences: "Today we have school, then after school we're going to [do X]. [Visit day / no visit today]." The children know what shape the day has. No surprises where possible.

steps.title: Begin the day.
steps.body: Breakfast, shoes, coats, out the door. The anchor is done. It does not expand into a conversation. It is 3 minutes.

contraindications: **Absolute:** if the morning is already in crisis (a child is sick, the mother is at the edge, there has been a fight or a meltdown — the anchor yields to the crisis response card)

**Caution:** if the mother is using the anchor to perform okayness she does not feel — the anchor should be genuine, even if it is a tired, quiet, minimal version. Performed calm is not the same as held calm.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In either child: refusal to come to the room (fine — anchor happens anyway, they hear it from wherever they are); crying or distress on waking (address the distress first, anchor after or skip); somatic complaints on waking (tummy ache, headache — attend to the child, then anchor or skip). - In yourself (the mother): unable to get the sentence out because grief is too close to the surface. This happens. On those mornings, the anchor can be silent — just stand in the kitchen, breathe once, say "okay, let's go" to the children. A minimal anchor is still an anchor. If this happens more mornings than not for two or more weeks, see mother-alone/re-centering/34-im-at-the-edge-escalation.md. - If any child signal or your own signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric/adult crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter (60 seconds):** Stand together. One breath. "Here we are. Today we have school." Done.

**"Hard morning" version:** No sentence, no breath exercise. Just: "Good morning. Here's breakfast." The fact that breakfast is served on time *is* the anchor. Some mornings the container is just the structure of the day itself.
**If a child is home sick:** the anchor happens at the child's bedside. One sentence. One breath. "We're here today. You're resting."

**Visit-day mornings:** the anchor adds one line: "Today we're going to see Daddy after [school/lunch]. We'll talk about what to expect later." Not more than that. Prep details come closer to visit time (see `father-with-kids-supervised/entry/01-beginning-ritual-quiet-arrival.md` for the visit-day prep frame).
traditions: - Jewish modeh ani — the morning gratitude prayer, recited upon waking. Mainstream Jewish practice, open, widely shared. (Reference: standard siddur; any introductory guide to Jewish prayer.) - Christian morning offering — a dedication of the day. Many forms across denominations. The Ignatian version is one of the most structured. (Reference: Fleming, What Is Ignatian Spirituality?, 2008.) - Buddhist metta — loving-kindness practice directed toward the family. Openly taught across Theravada and Mahayana schools. (Reference: Salzberg, Lovingkindness, 1995.) - Secular humanist — the spoken sentence as ritual in itself. (Reference: Sagan, For Small Creatures Such as We, 2019.)
The practice is offered as a universal form. The mother chooses (or does not choose) a tradition-specific interior practice to accompany it.

protocols.title: Bedtime Ritual — Non-Negotiable
protocols.energyCost: low (this must be sustainable on the mother's hardest days)
summary: Bedtime ritual is the single most evidence-supported daily intervention for children under stress. The attachment literature (Bowlby 1969/1982; Fiese et al. 2002) and the sleep research (Mindell et al. 2006 on bedtime routines and child sleep outcomes) converge: a consistent, predictable bedtime routine — same order, same words, same shape every night — is the primary daily secure base for young children. When everything else in the family is changing, bedtime does not change.

The word "non-negotiable" is intentional. This is the one practice in the kit that is not optional. Even on the worst night, even when the mother is exhausted, even after a hard visit, even when the children are dysregulated — bedtime happens in the same shape. The shape can be minimal (see Modifications) but the shape itself does not change.
This is the mother's domain. It should not be asked to stretch to accommodate the father's situation, to process grief, or to become a therapeutic activity. Its job is to be boring and reliable.

steps.title: Announce.
steps.body: "Ten minutes until teeth." Let the children finish what they are doing.

steps.title: Teeth and pajamas.
steps.body: Same order, same bathroom, same routine. No surprises.

steps.title: Into bed.
steps.body: Each child in their own bed (or together if they share a room). Comfort objects in place.

steps.title: One story OR one song.
steps.body:
Not both (unless the family has always done both). Pick one and keep it the same shape:
   - *Story*: one picture book, read aloud. The 5yo and 8yo can share the same book if they are in the same room, or each gets their own short book if they are in separate rooms (the mother may need to toggle between rooms — this is fine; the sequence still holds).
   - *Song*: one lullaby. The same lullaby every night, or the same 2–3 lullabies in rotation. The children's nervous systems will learn to associate this specific song with sleep. If the mother has a voice recording of the father singing a lullaby (from connection-capsule work — see `father-alone/connection-capsules/25-bedtime-story-voice-recording.md`), she may play it on some nights alongside her own singing. This is a quiet connection-capsule moment; it should not be heavy.

steps.title: One goodnight phrase.
steps.body:
The same phrase every night. Examples:
   - "I love you. You are safe. See you in the morning."
   - "Goodnight, sleep tight, I'll be right here."
   - "You are loved. Good night."
   Keep it short. Keep it the same. The children will begin to say it back.

steps.title: Lights out or lights dim.
steps.body: The mother stays for 30 seconds to a minute after the phrase, hand on the child's back or sitting on the edge of the bed. Then she leaves the room (or moves to the other child's room to repeat steps 3-6). The leaving is gentle, unhurried, and happens the same way every time.

steps.title: If the child calls back:
steps.body: one return, brief, same phrase again. After that, the mother stays nearby (in the hallway, in the next room) but does not re-enter for conversation. This boundary protects the child's sleep and the mother's evening.

contraindications: **This practice has no "when NOT to use" exceptions.** Bedtime happens every night. If the mother is incapacitated, another trusted adult does it in the same shape. If the family is traveling, the shape travels with them (same story, same song, same phrase, in a different room).

The only exception: if a child is in acute crisis (pediatric crisis signals from the crisis-response card — persistent nightmares more than 2 weeks, self-harm, statements of wanting to disappear) — then the bedtime ritual still happens, AND the crisis response path activates in parallel. Bedtime does not yield to crisis; it runs alongside it.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the 5yo: resistance to bed (normal — do not negotiate; the sequence continues), fear of the dark (nightlight, door ajar — add these to the routine so they are predictable), nightmares (waking screaming — go to him, hold him, say the goodnight phrase again, stay until he settles; if nightmares persist more than 2 weeks, see 00-crisis-response-card.md pediatric crisis signals). - In the 8yo: wanting to talk about Daddy at bedtime (receive briefly — "I hear you; let's talk about that tomorrow at [specific time]" — redirect to sleep), difficulty falling asleep (lying awake worrying — offer a simple body-based technique: "squeeze your toes, hold for 5 seconds, release; now your ankles; now your knees" — progressive relaxation in child-friendly form), or refusing to sleep because she is monitoring the mother's emotional state (parentification flag — "I am okay. You don't need to watch me. Your job is to sleep. I will be right here"). - In yourself (the mother): exhaustion so deep that the ritual feels impossible. On these nights, do the minimal version (see Modifications). If exhaustion is persistent across many nights, the container needs reinforcement — see mother-alone/re-centering/34-im-at-the-edge-escalation.md. - If any child signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Minimal version (5 minutes, for the mother's hardest nights):** Teeth and pajamas done. Into bed. No story, no song. Just: the goodnight phrase, hand on the back for 30 seconds, lights out. The phrase alone, said in the same voice, is enough to carry the anchor. This is not a failure. This is the floor of the practice, and the floor still holds.
**If one child is sick:** bedtime for the well child continues unchanged. The sick child gets a modified version at their bedside (phrase, hand on back, lights dim).

**If the mother has help (a grandmother, a trusted friend):** the helper can do the physical steps (teeth, pajamas) but the goodnight phrase and the hand-on-back should be the mother's if at all possible. The voice matters.
**Traveling:** bring the comfort objects. Say the same phrase. Sing the same song. The room is different; the ritual is not.
traditions: - The universal human practice of putting children to bed with words and touch — every culture does this. It is perhaps the most ancient form of daily ritual. - Jewish Shema before sleep — the most fundamental Jewish prayer, said at bedtime in many homes. Mainstream, open. (Reference: standard siddur.) - Christian bedtime prayer — widely practiced across denominations; "Now I lay me down to sleep" dates to the 18th century. (Reference: standard Christian devotional sources.) - Buddhist metta for the child — openly taught. (Reference: Salzberg, Lovingkindness, 1995.) - Secular humanist — the goodnight phrase is itself the ritual. No tradition required beyond the act of saying "you are loved" and meaning it.

The practice is offered as a universal form. Every family already knows how to do this. The kit's contribution is: keep doing it, the same way, every night, even when everything else is falling apart. Especially then.

protocols.title: After-School Decompression
protocols.duration: 15–20 minutes (the first stretch after arriving home from school)
summary: The transition from school to home is one of the highest-demand moments of a child's day. At school, children regulate their behavior for hours. When they walk through the door, they often decompress — loudly, messily, or by going quiet and flat. For children carrying the additional load of a parent with progressive cognitive change, this transition can be especially charged, particularly on visit days or the day after a visit.

The after-school decompression is not a therapy session. It is a deliberate 15-minute window in which the children are allowed to arrive home without being asked questions, given tasks, or processed. The mother provides a sensory anchor (snack, water, a quiet space) and waits. The children's nervous systems do the work.

steps.title: Arrive home.
steps.body: Coats off, shoes off, school bags put down.

steps.title: Snack and water on the table.
steps.body: "Here's your snack." No follow-up question.

steps.title: The 15-minute window.
steps.body: The mother is present but not asking. She can be in the kitchen, nearby, available. She does not initiate conversation. She does not ask about school. She does not bring up the visit. She does not ask "are you okay?" She is simply there.

steps.title: What the children do during this window is up to them:
steps.body:
- The 5yo may eat silently, then start playing on the floor. This is regulation.
   - The 5yo may start talking unprompted. Receive whatever comes without steering.
   - The 8yo may go to her room and close the door. This is regulation. It is not rejection.
   - The 8yo may want to talk immediately. Listen. Do not redirect to the visit or the father unless she raises it herself.
   - Either child may have a meltdown. This is also regulation — the home is the safe place where the held-together energy of the school day can finally come apart. Hold the meltdown without trying to fix it. Say "I'm here" and wait.

steps.title: After 15 minutes, one gentle opening.
steps.body: "How are you doing?" or "How was your heart today?" (borrowing from protocol `14-how-was-your-heart-today.md`). If the child answers, receive it. If the child says "fine" or shrugs, accept it. The decompression has done its work whether or not it produces conversation.

steps.title: Transition to homework, play, or the next part of the afternoon.
steps.body: The decompression is over. The day's structure resumes.

contraindications: **Absolute:** if the child arrives home in acute distress (crying, inconsolable, reporting something harmful that happened at school) — attend to the distress first. The decompression protocol yields to the child's need for immediate co-regulation.

**Caution:** on visit days or the day after a visit, the children may arrive home carrying more than usual. The decompression still applies — the 15-minute no-questions window is even more important when the child is carrying visit-related feelings. Do not rush to process the visit. Let the child decompress first.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the 5yo: meltdown that escalates beyond what a held "I'm here" can settle (tantrum lasting more than 15 minutes without any settling, physical aggression, self-harm). If this happens, the decompression yields to co-regulation: hold the child, slow breath together, and if the distress does not settle, see 15-emergency-co-regulation-90-seconds.md. - In the 8yo: completely silent withdrawal sustained across the afternoon (not just 15 minutes of quiet, but hours of not speaking, not eating, not engaging). A single quiet afternoon is normal; if it sustains across days, see 00-crisis-response-card.md pediatric crisis signals. - In yourself (the mother): anxiety about the silence (wanting to fill it, wanting to check, wanting to ask). The anxiety is information about your own state, not about the children's. Take a breath. The silence is the practice. If the anxiety sustains and you cannot be present without asking, you may need your own fast-reset first (see mother-alone/re-centering/33-fast-reset-sixty-seconds.md). - If any child signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter (5 minutes):** Snack on the table. No questions for 5 minutes. Then gently open. Even 5 minutes of no-questions is better than immediate interrogation.

**Visit-day variant:** On visit days, the decompression happens both before the visit (prep — see `father-with-kids-supervised/entry/01-beginning-ritual-quiet-arrival.md`) and after the visit (post-visit). The post-visit decompression may need to be longer (20–30 minutes) and may include physical activity (a walk, jumping on a trampoline, running in the yard) before any verbal processing. The body needs to discharge before the mind can organize.

**If the mother picks up at school and the children talk in the car:** let them talk. The car ride is a natural decompression space (no eye contact required; motion is regulating). When they arrive home, the 15-minute window still applies — the transition from car to home is its own moment.
traditions: - Quaker expectant waiting — sitting in silence without an agenda, trusting what arises. (Reference: Pink Dandelion, The Quakers: A Very Short Introduction, 2008.) - Zen concept of mu — not-doing, not-forcing — referenced philosophically as permission to be present without acting. (Reference: philosophical concept in Zen Buddhism, not a specific practice being taught.) - Universal parenting wisdom — the practice of not asking a child "how was your day?" the moment they walk through the door is recommended by developmental psychologists across frameworks. The 15-minute buffer is grounded in practical observation of child regulation patterns.

The practice is offered as a universal form. Every parent who has ever watched a child decompress after school already knows this rhythm. The kit's contribution is: name it, protect it, and don't let the family's larger stress compress it out of existence.

protocols.title: "Why Can't Daddy Live With Us?" — Scripts
protocols.duration: 2–5 minutes per conversation (not a long session — short, honest, done)
summary: The question will come. It may come as "why can't Daddy live with us?" or "when is Daddy coming home?" or "why does Daddy live somewhere else?" or "is Daddy going to die?" or "did I make Daddy sick?" Each of these is a variant of the same underlying question: what is happening to my family, and am I safe?

The research on children facing parental illness (Rauch & Muriel 2006; Christ 2000) consistently finds that what damages children is not the truth but the silence, evasion, and disorganization around the truth. Children who are told age-appropriate truths, said once, calmly, do better across every measured domain than children who are protected from knowing. The silence is worse than the answer.

This protocol provides scripts — not word-for-word prescriptions, but shaped responses the mother can adapt to her own voice. The scripts are said once, calmly, not repeated anxiously. If the child asks again later, the mother repeats the same answer in the same tone. Consistency, not elaboration, is what settles a child.
contraindications: **Absolute:** never at bedtime (the child needs to sleep, not process; if the question comes at bedtime, receive it briefly — "I hear you, and we'll talk about it tomorrow after breakfast" — then return to the bedtime ritual); never when the mother is actively crying or unable to regulate (the child reads the mother's state before the words; regulated first, then conversation)

**Caution:** never in front of the father during a visit (this is a mother-with-kids conversation, not a family-together one; the father should not witness the children processing his condition in real time — it is not his burden to absorb during his limited visit time); never when the other child is in the middle of their own distress (give each child their own space for this)
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the 5yo: crying (hold him; the answer was heard and is being processed through the body; do not add more words), asking the same question repeatedly in the same conversation (answer once more with the same words; then: "we can talk about it again tomorrow"), magical-thinking elaboration ("I'll make Daddy better by being good") — gently correct once: "nothing you do will make this different. You are already good enough." - In the 8yo: going very quiet after the conversation (processing; give her space; check in later), becoming angry at the mother ("why did you LET this happen?") — receive the anger without defending: "I hear you. You're angry. That makes sense." Asking for more detail than the mother has ("what exactly is wrong with his brain?") — be honest about what you know and don't know: "I don't have all the answers. Here's what I do know: [repeat the core frame]." - In yourself (the mother): Tears while speaking are normal and the children can see that you are human — pause, breathe, resume. But genuine dysregulation is different from tears. If you find yourself unable to speak, unable to stop crying, or losing the thread of what you are saying — this is your body telling you to pause. Say: "I need a minute. I love you. We will come back to this." Leave the room briefly if you can. Return within 5 minutes to close the conversation with one sentence: "I'm okay. We can talk more tomorrow." If you cannot return within 5 minutes, send a trusted adult to the child with the same message. Do not abandon the conversation entirely without closing it — a child left mid-conversation about a parent's illness will fill the silence with their own worst fears. Even a brief closing ("I love you, we're okay, we'll talk more") is enough. - If any child signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **If the child asks at a bad time (car, bedtime, in public):** "That's a really important question. Let's talk about it when we get home / tomorrow after breakfast." Then follow through. Do not break the promise.

**If both children ask at the same time:** answer the question for both, using the simpler version (the 5yo script). The 8yo can be given a private follow-up later with her own version. Do not make the 8yo wait too long.

**If the mother cannot say the words yet:** a family therapist or trusted adult can do the first conversation with the mother present. The mother's presence matters; her voice can come on the second round.

**If the father has been told not to discuss the diagnosis with the children (court or care-team guidance):** the mother carries this alone, which is heavy. She should have her own support (therapist, trusted friend) for debriefing after the conversation.
traditions: The practice of telling children the truth about difficult things, with love and without evasion, is universal. No specific tradition is drawn on here because the scripts are written in the family's own voice, not in any tradition's voice. The underpinning is developmental psychology (Rauch & Muriel 2006; Christ 2000) and attachment research (Main, Kaplan & Cassidy 1985 on coherent narrative as protective factor).

The one tradition worth naming: the Jewish concept of emet (truth) as a fundamental value — the Talmud teaches that the seal of God is truth (Shabbat 55a), and that truth, even when painful, creates the conditions for trust. The scripts above are an exercise in emet adapted for a 5-year-old and an 8-year-old.

protocols.title: "How Was Your Heart Today?"
summary: "How was your day?" asks a child to organize narrative. "How was your heart?" asks a child to check in with themselves. The difference matters. For children carrying the weight of a parent's progressive cognitive change, the narrative-organization question ("what happened?") is often too much. The felt-sense question ("how are you feeling?") is sometimes too abstract. "How was your heart?" sits between: it is concrete enough (the heart is a body part the child can point to) and open enough (the answer can be a word, a color, a gesture, or silence).

This is not an interrogation. It is a daily or weekly touchpoint that the mother offers, the children can accept or decline, and the response is received without judgment or correction. The child who says "my heart was gray today" is not asked to explain why. The answer is the whole answer.

steps.title: Ask.
steps.body: "How was your heart today?" Or a variant that fits the family's language: "How's your heart?" or "What color is your heart right now?" or "If your heart was weather, what would it be?"

steps.title: Wait.
steps.body: Do not fill the silence. The child may need 10–30 seconds to check in with themselves. That waiting is the practice.

steps.title: Receive.
steps.body:
Whatever the child says, receive it with one short acknowledgment:
   - "Thank you for telling me."
   - "I hear you."
   - "That makes sense."
   - A nod. A hand squeeze. A quiet "mm."

steps.title: Do not follow up unless the child invites it.
steps.body: If the child says "my heart was sad today" and then goes quiet, the practice is complete. The child has been heard. If the child says "my heart was sad today because of Daddy" and pauses — they may want to say more. Let them. If they do not, say "I'm glad you told me" and let it rest.

steps.title: The mother may share her own — briefly, to model, not to process.
steps.body: After the child has answered (or chosen not to), the mother can say: "My heart was [color/weather/word] today." This models that adults have hearts too, and that naming inner states is a normal human activity. Keep it honest but not heavy. "My heart was tired today" is enough. "My heart was drowning" is too much for a child to hold. **If the mother finds herself wanting to say more than one sentence about her own heart, that is information that she needs her own grief window** (see `mother-alone/container-practice/29-weekly-grief-window.md`), **not the children's ears.** The Absolute contraindication from Phase A §5.5 applies here: the children must never become the mother's emotional caregivers.

contraindications: **Absolute:** not at bedtime (bedtime is for sleep); not during or immediately after a supervised visit (too raw — use `15-emergency-co-regulation-90-seconds.md` if needed, or let the after-school decompression do its work first)
**Caution:** not when the mother herself is in a state where she cannot receive hard answers without crumbling — fast-reset first, then ask
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the 5yo: confusion about the question (reframe: "Is your body feeling happy or sad or something else right now?" — simpler, more concrete); answering "good" every time without variation (may be performing — that's okay; the practice plants the seed; the honest answers come later, on their own timeline). - In the 8yo: using the question to launch a long processing session (gently hold the boundary: "I hear you. Do you want to talk more, or is that enough for now?" — let her choose; if she chooses more, see 13-why-cant-daddy-live-with-us-script.md for the longer conversation frame); or deflecting with humor every time (also fine — humor is a legitimate coping strategy at 8; the practice plants the seed). - In yourself: if the child's answer surfaces your own grief (especially "my heart misses Daddy"), receive it, acknowledge it, and process your own response later — in your grief window, with a trusted friend, or in a private moment. Do not process your grief through the child's answer. "I miss him too" is honest. Breaking down in tears in response to the child's report is too much to put on the child. - If any child signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **For the 5yo who cannot use words:** "Show me with your hands — is your heart big or small today?" (Arms wide = big, arms close = small.) Or: "Pick a color for your heart right now." Or: "Draw your heart." (See `each-child-alone/age-5-boy/16-drawing-folder-he-owns.md`.)

**For the 8yo who wants more structure:** she may want to keep a small "heart weather" journal — one word or one weather symbol per day. See `each-child-alone/age-8-girl/19-simple-prompted-journal.md`.
