**If the mother cannot ask today:** skip it. The practice works cumulatively over weeks. Missing a day does not break it.
**Weekly version instead of daily:** if daily feels like too much, this can be a weekly check-in — Sunday evening, or after a visit, or whenever the family naturally pauses.
traditions: - The universal human practice of asking "how are you?" and meaning it — the heart-check is a refinement of what every caring adult does. It shifts the question from the head (narrative) to the heart (felt-sense), which is a small and powerful move. - Contemplative inquiry traditions — the question "how is your heart?" appears across traditions: the Ignatian Examen's attention to consolation and desolation (Fleming 2008), the Sufi practice of checking the state of the qalb (heart), and the Buddhist practice of checking in with the mind-heart (citta). Referenced philosophically only. The question itself is universal. - Jewish tradition of emet (truth) — the same value that grounds protocol 13 (the scripts). The heart-check honors truth by inviting the child to report honestly from their own interior.
The practice is offered as a universal form. The question is the mother's own — she may find her own words for it. The principle is: ask, wait, receive, do not fix.

protocols.title: Emergency Co-Regulation — 90 Seconds
summary: This is the fast-acting co-regulation practice for the whole family in hot moments — when the children are dysregulated after a visit, when a meltdown is escalating, when the mother is at the edge and needs to settle herself AND the children simultaneously, when something has just happened and everyone's nervous systems are activated.

The polyvagal research (Porges 2011; Dana 2018, 2020) describes how nervous systems regulate each other through face, voice, and breath. When one person in the room is settled, it creates the conditions for others to settle. The mother does not have to be fully regulated to lead this practice — she only has to be regulated enough to start. The practice itself does the rest.

90 seconds is chosen deliberately. Research on cortisol response curves suggests that a physiological stress response, once activated, begins to de-escalate within 60–90 seconds if the activation source stops and a calming signal is introduced. 90 seconds is long enough to shift the nervous system and short enough that a 5-year-old can tolerate it.

steps.title: Get close.
steps.body: The mother moves physically close to both children. If they are in different rooms, she brings them together or goes to the one most activated. She sits on the floor if they are on the floor. She kneels if they are standing. She matches their level.

steps.title: Touch.
steps.body: One hand on one child, one hand on the other. On the back, the shoulder, the arm, the knee — wherever the child will accept touch. If a child rejects touch right now, the mother puts her hand on her own knee near the child. Proximity without contact is still co-regulation.

steps.title: Hum.
steps.body: The mother starts humming. Not a specific song — a low, steady hum at a comfortable pitch. The vibration in her chest and throat sends a vagal signal to her own nervous system AND to any child whose body is touching hers. The children do not have to hum back. Many will start automatically.

steps.title: Breathe.
steps.body: While humming, the mother's exhale is long (the hum IS a long exhale). After 3–4 hums, she may shift to audible breathing — a slow exhale through the mouth, like a sigh. The children's breathing will start to entrain (match) hers within about 30 seconds. This is automatic and does not require their cooperation.

steps.title: Count silently to 90.
steps.body: Or just stay in the hum/breathe cycle until the room settles. The mother does not talk. She does not explain. She does not ask what happened. She hums, breathes, touches.

steps.title: When the 90 seconds are up
steps.body: (or when the room has visibly settled — whichever comes first), the mother says one quiet sentence: "I'm here. We're okay." Then she waits another 10 seconds. Then normal life can resume — a glass of water, a quiet activity, a return to whatever was happening before the activation.

contraindications: **This practice has almost no contraindications.** It is the floor of the kit — the practice that holds when nothing else does.

**The one exception:** if a child is in a state of physical aggression (hitting, kicking, throwing things AT someone), the mother may need to physically hold or redirect the child before co-regulation can begin. Co-regulation works only when the body is no longer fighting.

**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.** If the mother or a child is in acute crisis (self-harm, inability to function, thoughts of harm), the 90-second practice may help as a bridge to the escalation path, but it is not a substitute for crisis support.
signals: This practice does not have "stop" signals in the usual sense — it IS the stop signal for everything else. However:

- If a child pushes away from the mother's touch: do not force it. Hum without touch. Stay close. The child may return to contact after 20–30 seconds. - If the hum makes a child more distressed: (rare but possible — some children find humming overstimulating when very activated) switch to silent slow breathing only. Or whisper "shhh, shhh, shhh" rhythmically — same vagal pathway, different sound. - If the mother cannot hum because she is crying: quiet, steady tears with a long exhale are compatible with the practice — crying IS a long exhale with vocalization, and the children will feel the rhythm and begin to entrain. This is not failure; this is the practice working through tears. However, heaving sobs or hyperventilation are NOT compatible — sobs replace the long exhale with short gasps, defeating the vagal mechanism and potentially dysregulating the children further. If the mother's crying escalates from quiet tears to sobs: pause the hum, take one slow exhale through the mouth, attempt to restart the hum at a lower volume. If she cannot restart, use proximity and touch only (no vocalization) — hold the children close, breathe slowly, let the body do the settling. After the moment passes, she should seek her own co-regulation support (see mother-alone/re-centering/33-fast-reset-sixty-seconds.md or 34-im-at-the-edge-escalation.md). If the sobbing does not settle and the children's distress is escalating, activate the crisis response path (00-crisis-response-card.md) — call the trusted contact to come NOW. - If 90 seconds is not enough: do another 90 seconds. Or 3 minutes. Or as long as it takes. There is no upper limit on co-regulation. - If the activation does not settle after 5 minutes of continuous humming/breathing: the situation may require more than co-regulation. See 00-crisis-response-card.md. - If any child signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **30-second version (absolute minimum):** Sit. Touch. Three long exhales. "I'm here." Done.
**In the car:** no touch needed (children are in car seats). The mother hums or breathes audibly. The enclosed space amplifies the sound. The children's nervous systems will respond.

**At the visit location:** the mother, the children, and optionally the supervisor. The mother sits, gathers the children, hums. The supervisor stays still. The father may or may not be present; if he is, his body's presence (sitting quietly nearby) is itself part of the co-regulation field.

**Mother alone (no children present):** the same practice works for the mother by herself. Sit. Hum. 90 seconds. This is a fast-reset she can use in any moment — see `mother-alone/re-centering/33-fast-reset-sixty-seconds.md` for the solo version.

**For the 8yo who finds humming weird:** she does not have to hum. She can sit close, breathe, be still. Her nervous system will entrain to the mother's even without active participation. Tell her once: "You don't have to do anything. Just sit close for a minute."
traditions: - Polyvagal theory — Stephen Porges (2011) and Deb Dana (2018, 2020) — the scientific framework grounding why humming, long exhales, and touch produce nervous-system settling. Referenced as research, not as tradition. - Jewish niggun — wordless melody hummed in community, particularly Hasidic. Referenced philosophically: the mother's hum is structurally a niggun. (Reference: Hasidic musical tradition; widely shared.) - Buddhist om — sustained vocalization as meditation anchor. Referenced philosophically. (Reference: mainstream Buddhist and Hindu practice.) - Christian drone-singing — monastic chant traditions (Gregorian, Taizé). Referenced philosophically. (Reference: Taizé community music; Gregorian chant tradition.) - Universal human humming — every culture hums to soothe. Lullabies across all traditions are essentially structured humming with words. This practice strips the words and returns to the hum.
The practice is offered as a universal form. No tradition is required. The hum is the tradition — it is older than any lineage and belongs to every body that has a voice.

protocols.title: Drawing Folder He Owns
summary: At five, a child's primary expression modes are felt-sense, body, play, and drawing — not words. He does not yet have the narrative scaffolding to tell the story of what he feels; he will draw it, play it, or enact it through his body (Christ 2000; Cait 2005). Drawing-as-writing adapts Pennebaker's expressive writing paradigm (Pennebaker 1997; Fivush et al. 2007) to the developmental stage where pencil-on-paper IS the language.

The critical design principle: the folder is HIS. He controls what goes in, what comes out, what is shown, and what is kept private. The mother does not interpret the drawings back to him. She does not correct them, grade them, or display them without his permission. The folder is his interior life in visible form, and its privacy is his to manage.

steps.title: The folder is available.
steps.body: It lives where the child can reach it — on a low shelf, in a basket by his bed, on the kitchen table during drawing time.

steps.title: The child draws when he wants to.
steps.body: Not scheduled. Not prompted. Not assigned.

steps.title: If the mother is present when he draws:
steps.body: she sits nearby, available, not directing. She may draw her own picture alongside him (parallel drawing, like parallel play — the grown-up version of "I'm doing this too"). She does not ask "what are you drawing?" unless he invites her to look.

steps.title: If he shows her a drawing:
steps.body: she receives it. "Tell me about this" is better than "what is this?" (the first invites; the second demands). If he explains, she listens. If he says "it's a drawing," that is a complete answer.

steps.title: If a drawing is clearly about the father or the visits
steps.body: (a figure labeled Daddy, a building that looks like the visit location, a picture of the family with someone missing or changed): receive it without interpretation. Do not say "you miss Daddy, don't you?" Say "thank you for showing me." The drawing speaks for itself. If the content concerns the mother (drawings of harm, drawings of the child alone, repeated images of destruction), she may want to bring the folder to the family therapist for guidance — but she does not tell the child this. She simply notes it.

steps.title: The drawings stay in the folder
steps.body: unless the child takes them out. Over time, the folder accumulates — a visible record of his interior life across weeks and months. This is itself a connection-capsule: the drawings made at age 5, about this time in his life, will be meaningful to him at 15 and at 25 in ways neither he nor the mother can predict now.

contraindications: **Absolute:** do not use the drawings as diagnostic material without a child therapist's guidance — a 5-year-old's drawing of a dark sky is not necessarily a distress signal; it may be a dark sky. The mother watches patterns over weeks, not individual drawings.

**Caution:** do not ask the child to draw "what happened at the visit today" — that turns the folder into an assignment. If he draws it unprompted, receive it without interpretation. If he does not, the folder is not failing.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - Distress during drawing: if the child becomes upset while drawing (crying, tearing the paper, throwing crayons), the drawing has surfaced something. Do not take the drawing away. Say "I see you're having a hard time. Do you want to keep drawing, or do you want to stop?" Honor his answer. If he stops, the 90-second co-regulation practice (15-emergency-co-regulation-90-seconds.md) is available. - Repeated imagery of harm, destruction, or self-directed violence across multiple drawings over weeks: this is a pattern to bring to the family therapist, not a single-drawing alarm. One drawing of a monster eating a house is normal 5-year-old imagination. Ten drawings of monsters eating houses over three weeks is a message. - Refusal to draw: completely fine. Some children do not express through drawing. The folder waits. Other practices (sensory bin play, body-based co-regulation, bedtime story) carry the expressive work. - Magical-thinking self-blame: if a drawing reveals the child believes he caused the father's condition ("I drew Daddy sick because I was bad"), this is a 5-year-old's preoperational magical thinking, not a clinical emergency — but it needs a response. See mother-with-kids/processing-confusion/13-why-cant-daddy-live-with-us-script.md for the age-appropriate script: "It's not because of you. It's not something you can catch. It's not something you can fix." Said once, calmly. - If any signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **If the child prefers modeling clay or playdough to drawing:** the "folder" becomes a "box" and the medium shifts. The principle is the same: his creative expression, his ownership, no interpretation.

**If the child wants to draw WITH Daddy during a supervised visit:** this is a beautiful crossover with `08-shared-drawing-each-adds.md`. The drawing from the visit can go into the folder. A drawing made with Daddy is a connection-capsule.

**If the child cannot hold a crayon comfortably:** finger paints (washable, non-toxic) on large paper on the floor. Or stickers on paper. Or tearing colored paper and gluing it. The medium adapts; the ownership principle stays.
traditions: - Universal childhood drawing — every culture's children draw. The practice of giving a child paper and colors and stepping back is as old as pigment on cave walls. - Art therapy research — the principle that drawing externalizes interior states is grounded in clinical art therapy (Malchiodi 2011 [verification needed for specific citation]) and narrative therapy's externalization principle (White & Epston 1990; Freeman, Epston & Lobovits 1997). - Pennebaker's expressive writing paradigm — adapted for pre-literate children. (Reference: Pennebaker 1997; Fivush et al. 2007.)
The practice is offered as a universal form. The folder is the child's own.

protocols.title: Sensory Bin — Solo
summary: The sensory bin appears in the father-with-kids-supervised configuration as a shared activity (05-sensory-bin-play.md). This solo version serves a different purpose: it is a self-regulation tool the 5-year-old can reach for on his own, without needing an adult to initiate. When his body is activated — after a visit, after a hard moment, after waking from a nightmare, or just because 5-year-old nervous systems run hot — the bin gives his hands something to do and his attention something to settle on.
The sensory engagement is the regulation mechanism. He does not have to name what he feels. He pours, stirs, scoops, buries, finds. His nervous system does the rest.

steps.title: The child goes to the bin when he wants to.
steps.body: No adult initiates.

steps.title: He plays.
steps.body: Pours, stirs, scoops, buries, finds. No goal. No right way.

steps.title: The mother is nearby but not directing.
steps.body: She does not ask "what are you making?" She does not join unless invited. She is available.

steps.title: He stops when he stops.
steps.body: Puts the lid on. The practice is done.

steps.title: Cleanup
steps.body: is putting the lid on and sweeping any spilled filler with a small brush he keeps next to the bin. The cleanup is part of the practice — it is a small concrete action that closes the cycle.

contraindications: **Absolute:** the child puts filler in his mouth — stop, redirect, and assess whether this bin is appropriate for him right now. If hand-to-mouth behavior persists, replace the bin with a water-play version (a bowl of warm water with cups — no choking hazard).

**Caution:** if the child uses the bin to throw filler at people or furniture, the practice has shifted from regulation to dysregulation — the bin is paused for the day and a co-regulation practice (`15-emergency-co-regulation-90-seconds.md`) may be needed first.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - Escalating energy (throwing filler, slamming cups, yelling at the bin): the regulation has tipped into dysregulation. The mother gently says "let's close the bin for now" and offers an alternative (held body, a walk outside, the 90-second co-regulation hum). - Hand-to-mouth behavior: immediate redirect. If persistent, swap to water play. - Complete disinterest over weeks: fine. Not every child regulates through tactile play. The bin waits. Other practices carry the work. - Magical-thinking self-blame: if the child's play or words during bin time reveal self-blame about the father's condition ("I'm making Daddy's rocks sad"), see mother-with-kids/processing-confusion/13-why-cant-daddy-live-with-us-script.md for the age-appropriate response: "It's not because of you." - If any signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Water version (no choking concern):** a small bowl of warm water with 2 cups and a sponge. Same regulation principle, safer for persistent hand-to-mouth children.
**Outdoor version:** a patch of dirt, sand, or gravel in the yard. The earth IS a sensory bin. A stick and a small bucket are the tools.
**Bedtime version:** a very small bag of smooth stones (5–6 stones, each larger than 4 cm) that the child holds and sorts in bed before sleep. Calming, tactile, no filler to spill.
traditions: - Early childhood education — Montessori and Reggio Emilia sensory-exploration traditions - Snoezelen principle — low-demand sensory engagement as regulation (Hulsegge & Verheul 1987)

The practice is offered as a universal form. Every child who has ever dug in sand or stirred in a bowl already knows this practice. The kit's contribution is: name it as a regulation tool, make it accessible, and protect it.

protocols.title: Bedtime Story from Daddy's Voice
protocols.level: middle (requires a connection-capsule voice recording to exist)
summary: If the father has recorded himself reading a bedtime story (see father-alone/connection-capsules/25-bedtime-story-voice-recording.md), the recording becomes a bridge between homes and across time. The 5-year-old hears his father's voice — the specific prosody, breath pattern, and warmth that his nervous system imprinted on from infancy — without requiring the father to be physically present. The voice recording draws on the same procedural-memory channel that songs use (Sacks 2007): the sound of Daddy reading is recognized by the body before the mind.

This practice is optional. It depends on whether a connection-capsule recording exists. If no recording exists, the practice does not apply — the mother's own bedtime reading (11-bedtime-ritual-non-negotiable.md) carries the work.

steps.title: Standard bedtime ritual in progress
steps.body: (teeth, pajamas, into bed, comfort objects — per `11-bedtime-ritual-non-negotiable.md`).

steps.title: The mother says:
steps.body: "Do you want to hear Daddy's story tonight?" A yes/no question. The child chooses.

steps.title: If yes:
steps.body: play the recording at a gentle volume. The child listens. The mother sits nearby — she does not leave the room while the recording plays.

steps.title: While the recording plays:
steps.body: the mother's hand on the child's back, or nearby. Her physical presence bridges the gap between the recorded voice and the live moment. The child is hearing Daddy AND feeling Mommy. Both are present.

steps.title: When the recording ends:
steps.body: the mother does not ask "how was that?" She continues with the standard bedtime sequence — the goodnight phrase, the hand on the back, lights out. The recording is absorbed into the ritual, not separated from it.

steps.title: If the child cries or becomes activated:
steps.body: the mother says "I know. I hear you." She stays. She may hum (per `15-emergency-co-regulation-90-seconds.md`). She does not turn the recording off mid-story unless the child asks. Hearing the story through tears is still hearing the story.

contraindications: **Absolute:** if no recording exists (do not manufacture this need — the bedtime ritual holds without it); if the father has withdrawn consent for the recording's use; if the child has explicitly asked not to hear it ("I don't want Daddy's story tonight")
**Relative:** on nights when the child is already in acute distress (the recording may deepen the distress rather than settle it — use the mother's own voice and the standard bedtime ritual instead)

**Caution:** on the first few uses, the child may react with tears, excitement, confusion, or clinging. These are all normal responses to hearing the absent parent's voice. The mother holds the space. If the tears do not settle within 5 minutes, the recording is paused and the bedtime ritual continues without it.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - The child begins to refuse the recording consistently (asks "no Daddy story" more nights than not): honor it without question. The recording may return later, at a different age, or never. Both are fine. - The child becomes more activated by the recording than settled (increased nightmares on recording nights, increased clinginess, increased "I want Daddy" distress that does not settle): pause the recording for two weeks. Return to the mother's own bedtime voice only. If the pattern repeats on reintroduction, the recording may not be serving this child at this stage. - The child wants to hear it on repeat (asks for the same story three times): allow it for a time. Repetition is how 5-year-olds process. If the repetition becomes compulsive (cannot sleep without it, escalating if it is not available), the mother may gently introduce a limit: "one Daddy story, then one Mommy story, then sleep." - Magical-thinking self-blame: if the child says things like "Daddy sounds different because I was bad" or "I want to fix Daddy's voice," see mother-with-kids/processing-confusion/13-why-cant-daddy-live-with-us-script.md for the age-appropriate response. Said once, calmly: "It's not because of you. It's not something you can catch, like a cold. It's not something you can fix." - If any signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **If no recording exists:** the mother reads the same book the father would have read, if she knows which one. Or she reads any book in her own voice. The bedtime ritual does not depend on the recording.
**If the child wants to hear the recording during the day (not at bedtime):** allow it. The recording is a comfort object, like a stuffed animal. It can be accessed at the child's initiative.

**For the 8yo:** she may want to listen on her own, privately, with headphones. Allow it. She may want to listen with her brother, or alone. Both are fine. Her developmental stage means she may process the recording more cognitively ("Daddy sounds different" / "I remember when he read this to me") — the mother receives whatever she says without steering.

**If the father's voice has changed noticeably since the recording was made:** the recording becomes more precious, not less. It preserves a specific moment. The child does not need to be told "Daddy used to sound like this" — the recording speaks for itself.
traditions: - The universal human practice of storytelling at bedtime — older than any single tradition - Japanese monogatari — story-as-gift, story-as-legacy, voice carrying what presence cannot - Dignity Therapy research (Chochinov et al. 2005) — the connection-capsule concept adapted from adult palliative care to younger-onset family context, with the ethical constraint that the father is never asked to perform his own decline
The practice is offered as a universal form. The father's voice is its own tradition.

protocols.title: Simple Prompted Journal
summary: At eight, the child is entering concrete operational thinking (Piaget 1952) and her narrative capacity is emerging — she can tell a story about her feelings, use metaphor, and hold cause-and-effect. She is beginning to need an interior life that is hers and not shared with the adults. The journal serves this need.

James Pennebaker's expressive writing paradigm (Pennebaker 1997; 200+ studies) shows that brief, structured writing about emotionally significant experiences is associated with improved cognitive integration and reduced physiological stress markers. The child-adaptation literature (Fivush et al. 2007) suggests similar benefits for school-age children when writing is prompted, private, and ungraded.

The journal is private unless she chooses to share. This is non-negotiable. The mother does not read it. The mother does not ask to see it. If the child shows a page, the mother receives it. If the child never shows anything, the journal is still working.

steps.title: The journal is available.
steps.body: It lives where she can reach it — her room, her backpack, her bedside.

steps.title: She writes when she wants to.
steps.body: Not scheduled. Not prompted unless she asks.

steps.title: If she asks "what should I write?"
steps.body:
, the mother offers ONE prompt from the list below and steps back:
   - "One thing I want to remember about today."
   - "One thing I wish was different."
   - "One thing I want to tell someone but haven't yet."
   - "If my heart was weather today, it would be ____."
   - "A letter I might never send."
   - "Three things I noticed today."
   - "Something I'm good at."
   - "Something that made me laugh this week."

steps.title: If she shows the mother a page:
steps.body: the mother reads it, says "thank you for showing me," and does not interpret, correct, grade, or redirect. If the content is painful ("I wrote about how I miss Daddy"), the mother says "I hear you. Thank you for writing that." If the content is light ("I wrote about my cat"), the mother says "I love that." Both are received equally.

steps.title: The journal accumulates.
steps.body: Over months and years, it becomes a record of her interior life during this chapter of her family's story. At 18, at 25, she may want to read it. She may not. Either way, it is hers.

contraindications: **Absolute:** do not use the journal as an assignment (no "write three sentences about your day" as homework — this turns the journal into a school obligation and kills its expressive function); do not read the journal without the child's explicit consent (including not "peeking" when she is not looking — children are acutely sensitive to privacy violations and will stop writing if they discover the journal is not safe)

**Relative:** if the child has significant writing anxiety from school (perfectionism, fear of mistakes, handwriting stress), the journal may import that anxiety. In that case, offer drawing alongside or instead — see `16-drawing-folder-he-owns.md` adapted for an 8yo, or a "heart weather" chart (one symbol per day, no sentences required).

**Caution:** if the child begins writing extensively about self-harm, dying, or wanting to disappear, this is a signal to loop in the family therapist — NOT to confiscate the journal or to confront the child about the content. The journal is surfacing something real; the response is professional support, not punishment.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - She stops writing: fine. The journal waits. Some children write in bursts (around hard moments) and go quiet (when things settle). The pattern itself is information. - She writes only performative content ("today was great! everything is fine!" every entry): this may be the 8yo performing okayness she thinks the adults want. It is not a crisis signal, but the mother should ensure the child knows the journal is truly private and that messy feelings are allowed. One gentle conversation: "Your journal is only for you. You don't have to write what you think I want to hear. You can write what's really going on, or you can write nothing. Both are okay." - She becomes anxious about the journal (worrying about it being read, carrying it everywhere, checking that it's safe): reassure her privacy once ("nobody reads this but you"), and then stop reassuring — over-reassurance makes the child more vigilant, not less. The boundary is set; let it hold. - Parentification in journal content (writing about taking care of her brother, worrying about her mother, monitoring adult emotional states): this is a message the family therapist should see — not the journal itself, but the pattern the mother observes. "She seems to be writing a lot about taking care of others" is something the therapist can work with. - If any signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Drawing + writing hybrid:** some 8-year-olds prefer to draw on one page and write on the facing page. Allow it. The journal's form adapts to her.

**"Heart weather" chart (lower-demand version):** one page with a row of boxes for each day of the week. She puts one weather symbol in each box (sun, cloud, rain, storm, fog, rainbow). No sentences. Over weeks, patterns emerge. She can share the chart or keep it private.

**Digital version:** if she prefers typing on a device, a simple notes app with a folder she controls serves the same function. Privacy concerns apply equally — password-protect or use a device only she accesses.

**Visit-day journal:** after a supervised visit, one prompted entry: "One thing I noticed about Daddy today." Optional. Not required. If she writes it, it goes in the regular journal, not a separate "visit journal."
traditions: - Pennebaker's expressive writing paradigm — 200+ studies on structured writing and health outcomes, adapted for children. (Reference: Pennebaker 1997; Fivush et al. 2007.) - Narrative therapy (White & Epston 1990) — the principle that writing one's experience changes one's relationship to it. - Diary traditions across cultures — Anne Frank, Sei Shōnagon (The Pillow Book, 10th-century Japan — a woman's private record of observations, feelings, and impressions), and countless unnamed diarists across history. The journal is one of the oldest forms of self-authored interior life. - Jewish tradition of emet (truth) — the same value grounding protocol 13 (the scripts). The journal honors truth by giving the child a place to be honest with herself.
The practice is offered as a universal form. The journal is the child's own.

protocols.title: Protected One-on-One with Mom
summary: The 8-year-old girl in this family carries a specific risk: parentification. Under stress, she may become the watcher — monitoring her mother's emotional state, caring for her 5-year-old brother, suppressing her own needs to avoid burdening the adults (Chase 1999; Earley & Cushway 2002). One of the most direct protections against parentification is protected time with her mother that is not about Daddy, not about her brother, not about the family situation. Just her and her mom, doing something she chose, with her mother's undivided attention.
This practice exists to communicate one message: "You matter. Not as a helper. Not as a second parent. As an 8-year-old girl whose mother wants to be with her."

steps.title: At the scheduled time, the 5yo goes to the trusted adult.
steps.body: The mother and the 8yo are alone.

steps.title: "What do you want to do today?"
steps.body: The child decides. The mother follows.

steps.title: Be together.
steps.body: No agenda. No outcome. No teaching. Just be with her. If she wants to talk, listen. If she wants to be quiet, be quiet with her. If she wants to be silly, be silly. If she cries, hold her. The hour is hers.

steps.title: Do not bring up Daddy, the visits, her brother, or the family situation
steps.body: unless she does. If she does, receive it and then gently check: "Do you want to keep talking about this, or do you want to go back to [what we were doing]?" Let her choose.

steps.title: End at the agreed time.
steps.body: "That was our time. I loved being with you. Same time next [day]." The closing is warm, brief, and carries the promise of next time.

contraindications: **Absolute:** the practice is NOT cancelled for convenience. It is only cancelled for genuine emergency (illness, safety issue). If cancelled, it is rescheduled within the same week and the child is told when. Cancelling this practice repeatedly teaches the child that her needs are the first to be set aside — which is exactly the parentification message this practice exists to prevent.

**Caution:** if the child consistently chooses activities where she "takes care of" the mother (baking FOR the family, cleaning the house together, helping with errands), the mother gently redirects: "this is YOUR time. What do you want to do that's just for you?" Caretaking during protected one-on-one is a parentification signal, not a preference.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - The child declines the practice ("I don't want to do our time this week"): accept it once. If it repeats, gently explore: "Is everything okay? You don't have to tell me, but I want you to know I love our time together." If she is declining because she feels she should be watching her brother or helping the mother, that is a parentification signal to address directly: "Your brother is safe with [trusted adult]. This time is yours." - The child uses the practice to process heavy content (disclosure of something at school, sudden grief about the father, a hard question): receive it. The one-on-one is a safe space for this. But do not let the processing consume every week — if the child consistently uses the hour for grief work, she may need a separate processing channel (family therapist, the heart-check in 14-how-was-your-heart-today.md), and the one-on-one should be protected as a joy-and-connection space. - The mother finds the practice emotionally difficult (grief at the comparison between this protected hour and the intact-family life she imagined): this is expected. The mother processes her own grief in her own channels (grief window, therapist, peer), not during the child's protected time. - If any signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter (20 minutes):** if 60 minutes is genuinely impossible to schedule weekly, 20 focused minutes is better than no protected time. The minimum viable practice is: the 5yo is elsewhere, the phone is away, and the mother's attention is undivided for 20 minutes.

**If no trusted adult is available for the 5yo:** the practice can happen after the 5yo's bedtime — the mother and the 8yo stay up 30 minutes later together. Not ideal (the 8yo is tired too) but functional.
**If the child wants to do something the mother does not enjoy:** do it anyway. This is her hour. The mother's job is to be present, not to be entertained.
**If the child wants to do nothing:** sit together. Read side by side. Lie on the couch. Doing nothing together is a complete practice.
traditions: - The universal parenting practice of dedicated attention — every culture recognizes that children need to be seen individually, not only as part of a group. The practice of "special time" or "one-on-one time" is recommended across developmental-psychology frameworks (Ginott, Gordon, Siegel & Bryson). - Ubuntu philosophy — "I am because we are" — the child's personhood is relational, and the one-on-one with her mother is where her own personhood (not her role as helper or watcher) is confirmed. - Winnicott's holding environment — the practice IS the holding environment for this child. The mother's undivided attention, predictably given, is the structure that allows the child's interior life to develop. (Reference: Winnicott, Playing and Reality, 1971.)

The practice is offered as a universal form. Every mother and every daughter already know this practice in some version. The kit's contribution is: protect it, schedule it, and do not let the family's crisis compress it out of existence.

protocols.title: Externalizing the Forgetting
protocols.level: middle (requires the child to have some stability first — not a first-week practice)
protocols.duration: 5–15 minutes (a single conversation, repeated as needed)
summary: Narrative therapy's central move — externalizing the problem — reframes a difficulty as something separate from the person, something the person is in relationship with rather than identical to (White & Epston 1990). For this family, the move is explicit: the Forgetting is not Daddy. The Forgetting is something that is happening to Daddy, that the family is living alongside.

This grammatical shift gives the child a relationship-with rather than a fusion-with. She can love Daddy AND dislike the Forgetting in the same sentence. She can be angry at the Forgetting without being angry at Daddy. She can notice when the Forgetting is stronger and when it is weaker without feeling that Daddy himself is appearing and disappearing. The externalization creates space between the person and the condition — space the child can breathe in.

The child-focused narrative therapy literature (Freeman, Epston & Lobovits 1997) provides many examples of children working with externalized "problems" through naming, drawing, and storytelling. At 8, the child is developmentally ready for this — she can hold metaphor, understand cause and effect, and use language to reshape her experience.

steps.title: The mother introduces the concept.
steps.body:
At a calm moment, when the child is receptive:

   "I want to tell you something that might help us talk about what's happening with Daddy. You know how Daddy's brain works differently now — how he forgets things and gets confused sometimes? Some people call that 'the Forgetting.' The Forgetting is something that's happening TO Daddy. It's not WHO Daddy is. Daddy is still Daddy — he still loves you, he still thinks you're amazing, he's still your dad. The Forgetting is like... [choose a metaphor that fits the child's world]:
   - ...like fog that comes and goes. Sometimes the fog is thick and Daddy can't see through it. Sometimes the fog lifts and Daddy is right there, clear as day.
   - ...like a bully that picks on Daddy's brain. The bully makes it hard for Daddy to remember things. But the bully is not Daddy.
   - ...like a glitch in a video game. The game is still the game, but sometimes it glitches."

steps.title: Wait.
steps.body:
Let the child respond however she responds. She may:
   - Ask questions ("Can we get rid of the Forgetting?" — "The doctors are trying to help. We can't get rid of it, but we can learn to live with it.")
   - Be silent (processing)
   - Cry (hold her)
   - Reject it ("That's dumb") — accept the rejection without defending the concept. It may land later.
   - Add to it ("I think the Forgetting is like a cloud" — "Tell me more about that.")

steps.title: If the concept lands,
steps.body:
the mother can use it going forward:
   - Before a visit: "The Forgetting might be strong today, or it might be quiet. We'll see."
   - After a visit: "How was the Forgetting today?" (This is different from "how was Daddy?" — it separates the condition from the person.)
   - In general conversation: "The Forgetting was making it hard for Daddy to find words today. But did you see how he smiled when you sang?"

steps.title: If the child wants to draw the Forgetting:
steps.body: let her. The drawing externalizes the externalization — it gives the concept a visual form the child controls. She can draw it as a fog, a cloud, a monster, a glitch, a gray blob. The drawing goes in her journal or her drawing folder if she wants.

steps.title: The concept is hers to use or not.
steps.body: The mother does not force the language. If the child prefers "Daddy's brain thing" or "the stuff that makes Daddy confused" or her own name for it, that is her externalization and it works just as well.

contraindications: **Absolute:** not with the 5-year-old without therapist guidance — the 5yo's preoperational thinking may confuse the externalization with magical thinking ("the Forgetting is a monster I can fight"), which could increase anxiety rather than reduce it. The externalization is designed for the 8yo's emerging concrete-operational capacity. If the 5yo overhears and asks, a simple version is possible: "Daddy's brain has something called the Forgetting. It's not Daddy's fault and it's not your fault." But do not elaborate without professional guidance.

**Caution:** if the child becomes anxious about the Forgetting as a "thing" (imagining it as a real entity, having nightmares about it), the externalization is not serving her and should be retired. Return to simpler framing: "Daddy's brain works differently now."

**"Will the Forgetting win?"** — in a progressive condition, the child will eventually ask whether the Forgetting will get stronger, whether Daddy can beat it, or whether there is a cure. This question requires clinical precision and emotional care simultaneously. A prepared honest response: "The Forgetting will probably get stronger over time. And Daddy is still Daddy underneath it. And we will keep finding ways to be with him, even when the Forgetting makes things harder. Nobody knows exactly what will happen, and we don't have to figure it all out right now." If the child presses for more certainty than you can honestly give, it is okay to say: "I don't know, sweetheart. What I do know is that we love him, and we're going to keep showing up." Do not promise a cure. Do not deny the progression. Do not make the child responsible for fighting the Forgetting. If this conversation surfaces significant distress in the child, loop in the family therapist.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - The child becomes anxious about the Forgetting as a real entity (scared of it, nightmares about it, believing she can "catch" it): the metaphor is not serving her. Retire it gently: "You know what, we don't have to call it that. Daddy's brain just works differently, and that's okay to say." - The child uses the Forgetting to express anger at the father ("I hate the Forgetting AND I hate Daddy"): this is okay in the short term. The child is allowed to be angry. The mother can gently separate: "It makes sense to be angry at the Forgetting. And Daddy is still Daddy underneath it." If the anger at the father specifically sustains and intensifies, loop in the family therapist. - The 5yo hears the concept and becomes confused or frightened: redirect to simpler language for the younger child. The externalization belongs to the 8yo's cognitive world. - If any signal sustains beyond this session, see pediatric crisis signals in 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **If the child prefers a different name:** the concept does not have to be called "the Forgetting." It can be "the Fog," "the Glitch," "the Thing," "the Brain Bully," or whatever the child names it. The naming is itself part of the externalization — she chooses what to call it, which gives her agency over the concept.
**If the mother prefers not to use externalization:** the scripts in `13-why-cant-daddy-live-with-us-script.md` work without it. The externalization is an additional tool, not a required frame.

**For use with the family therapist:** the therapist may want to develop the externalization further in sessions — drawing the Forgetting, writing letters to the Forgetting, having the child interview the Forgetting. These are narrative-therapy techniques that work best with professional guidance (Freeman, Epston & Lobovits 1997). The mother's role at home is the simpler version: use the language if the child uses it, and do not push.
traditions: - Narrative therapy (White & Epston 1990; Freeman, Epston & Lobovits 1997) — the externalization technique is drawn directly from this therapeutic tradition, which is open, published, and widely taught to clinicians and educators. - Universal storytelling — the practice of naming a difficulty as a character (the dragon, the wolf, the storm) is among the oldest narrative devices in human storytelling. Every tradition has monsters. The child who names her father's condition as "the Forgetting" joins a very old human practice of making the unspeakable speakable by giving it a name.
The practice is offered as a universal form, grounded in specific clinical technique (narrative therapy) and the universal human practice of naming the unnamed.

protocols.title: Daily Dignity Rhythm
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.3) identifies sensory anchoring practices as the primary activity menu for a father whose narrative memory is thinning but whose body, breath, and senses remain vital. The synthesis names sound, water, movement, and breath as channels that "work WITH the still-vital body" and draws on research showing that procedural and sensory memory persist when declarative memory does not (Sacks, 2007; Porges, 2011). A single recurring anchor done the same way every day may help build a rhythm the body recognizes even when the mind does not narrate it. Consistency over novelty. The same practice, the same time, the same place. This is not a therapeutic exercise prescribed to a patient. It is a practice chosen by a person.
contraindications: **DRIVING FLAG (Absolute):** This practice itself does not involve driving. However, per §5.1, driving status must be verified with the care team at each stage of the condition. No protocol in this kit assumes driving capacity.

**SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** The younger-onset dementia literature (Draper et al. 2010; Erlangsen et al. 2008) documents elevated suicide risk in the immediate post-diagnosis window and at major capability-loss transitions. If you are experiencing thoughts of self-harm, or if your caregiver notices acute distress, withdrawal, or hopelessness, this practice yields to crisis response. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.
**Situational:** If today is a day of acute confusion, agitation, or distress, do not force the practice. Let it go. It will be here tomorrow.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: increasing agitation during the practice, pacing instead of settling, tearfulness that will not ease, confusion about where he is or what he is doing, flat affect that feels unlike quiet settling. - What to do: Stop the practice gently. If a caregiver is present, they may offer water, a warm drink, or quiet company. No explanation is needed. The practice can be tried again tomorrow. If the distress is acute — thoughts of self-harm, inability to settle across hours, withdrawal from all engagement — activate the crisis response path. See 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter version (2 minutes):** Do only 5 breath cycles (Option A), or one round of hand-washing (Option B), or one face-touch with wet hands (Option C), or notice only three body parts — head, hands, feet (Option D).

**Lower-energy version (seated or lying down):** All four options can be done sitting in a chair or lying in bed. The standing body scan becomes a lying body scan. The hand-washing can be done with a warm washcloth brought to the bedside.
**"Hard day" version:** Simply sit in the spot where the practice usually happens. Breathe. Nothing more is required. Being in the spot counts.

**Caregiver-assisted version:** The caregiver prepares the space (fills the water bowl, sets the timer, puts on quiet music if that is part of the routine). The caregiver may say, "It's time for your practice" or simply guide the father to his spot. The caregiver stays nearby but does not instruct. If the father cannot initiate, the caregiver may begin the first gesture — pour the first cup of water, place the father's hands on his own knees — and then step back.
traditions: - Zen Buddhism — breath counting (susokukan) as foundational awareness practice. (Shunryu Suzuki, Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind, 1970.) - Judaism — netilat yadayim, morning hand-washing as daily renewal of agency. (Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 4:1.) - Islam — wudu ablution as sensory and spiritual preparation, adapted here at the level of water-and-presence rather than as religious obligation. (al-Ghazali, Ihya Ulum al-Din, Book 3.) - Secular somatic tradition via Buddhist vipassana — body-scan practice adapted by Jon Kabat-Zinn's MBSR program. (Kabat-Zinn, Full Catastrophe Living, 1990.) Standing version draws on Chinese qigong zhan zhuang tradition.

protocols.title: Fifteen-Minute Pre-Visit Centering
protocols.duration: 15 minutes (before leaving for a supervised visit)
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.3, §3.5, §5.1) names the capacity to "arrive settled" as a core need for the father before supervised visits. Polyvagal theory (Porges, 2011; Dana, 2018) proposes that the ventral vagal complex supports social engagement and calm connection through face, voice, and breath, and that this system may still function when declarative memory does not. Settling the nervous system before a social engagement is not the same as meditation. It is closer to what athletes call a pre-game routine: the body entering a state it recognizes, so the person can be present rather than braced. This practice may help the father arrive at the visit with his nervous system closer to calm, which in turn may help his children's nervous systems settle in his presence.

steps.title: Fifteen minutes before it is time to leave for the visit,
steps.body: the caregiver (or the father himself) says: "It's almost time. Let's sit for a few minutes first."

steps.title: Go to the quiet room.
steps.body: The father sits in his chair or his spot. The door can be open or closed — his preference.

steps.title: The warm drink is already there.
steps.body: He picks it up. He holds it. He feels the warmth in his hands. He takes a sip. There is nothing else to do with the drink except hold it and sip it.

steps.title: If music is part of the routine, it is already playing.
steps.body: One familiar song or a short playlist (no more than 15 minutes). Not new music. Not a podcast. Not the news. Something his body already knows.

steps.title: Sit.
steps.body: That is the instruction. Sit and drink the warm drink and listen to the music or the silence. If thoughts come about the visit — worry, anticipation, sadness, hope — let them come. They do not need to be managed.

steps.title: Three slow breaths.
steps.body: At some point in the fifteen minutes — it does not matter when — take three slow breaths. Inhale through the nose. Exhale through the mouth, longer than the inhale. The long exhale activates the parasympathetic nervous system (Dana, 2018). That is the only "technique" in this practice. Three breaths. The rest is sitting.

steps.title: When it is time to leave, someone says so.
steps.body: "It's time." The father finishes the drink or sets it down. He stands. He goes to the visit. The centering is done.

contraindications: **DRIVING FLAG (Absolute):** Per §5.1, driving status must be verified with the care team. This practice is done at home before leaving for the visit. It does not assume the father drives himself.

**SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** Elevated suicide risk in the post-diagnosis window and at capability-loss transitions (Draper et al. 2010; Erlangsen et al. 2008). If the father is in acute distress before a visit — tearfulness that will not settle, expressed hopelessness, statements about not wanting to go on — the visit itself should be evaluated with the care team. The centering practice does not override crisis response. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**Situational:** If the father is acutely agitated, confused about the day, or unable to settle in the room, do not force the practice. The caregiver may offer a simpler version (see Modifications) or simply sit with him quietly until it is time to leave.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: increasing agitation instead of settling, pacing, getting up and sitting back down repeatedly, tearfulness he cannot ease, statements of dread or refusal, flat shutdown (staring, unresponsive), confusion about what the visit is or who the children are. - What to do: The caregiver enters the room gently. Offers presence, not correction. "I'm here. We don't have to rush." If the father cannot settle within the fifteen minutes, the caregiver contacts the visit supervisor to discuss whether a brief delay is possible, or whether today's visit should be shorter. If the distress is acute, the crisis response path activates before the visit happens. See 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter version (5 minutes):** Warm drink, three slow breaths, sit. That is all.
**Lower-energy version:** The father does not leave the chair he is already in. The caregiver brings the warm drink to him. The music plays in the room he is already in.
**"Hard day" version:** No music. No instruction. The caregiver sits near the father and drinks their own warm drink. Two people sitting in a room. The co-regulation happens through shared quiet.

**Caregiver-assisted version:** The caregiver prepares the room in advance — warm drink ready, music queued, chair arranged. The caregiver guides the father to the room. If the father needs prompting for the three breaths, the caregiver can simply take three slow breaths themselves — the mirror neuron pathway may invite the father to follow without verbal instruction.
traditions: - Polyvagal theory (Stephen Porges, 2011; Deb Dana, 2018) — the neurophysiological basis for long-exhale breathing as parasympathetic activation. - Japanese tea ceremony — the principle that preparation before an encounter is itself part of the encounter. The host prepares the room, the water, the vessel before the guest arrives. (Okakura, The Book of Tea, 1906; Sen Soshitsu XV, Tea Life, Tea Mind, 1979.) - Quaker expectant waiting — the practice of sitting in silence not as absence but as presence. (Pink Dandelion, The Quakers: A Very Short Introduction, 2008.)

protocols.title: Morning Sensory Anchor
summary: Kate Swaffer's concept of "prescribed disengagement" (Swaffer, 2016) names the social prescription that newly diagnosed people should retire, rest, and prepare to die rather than be offered meaningful engagement. The Phase A synthesis (§3.1, §3.3, §5.1) explicitly names this as a harm younger-onset families can resist: "the father is a person with a diagnosis, not a diagnosis with a person attached." This practice is the counter-move. It is something the father does FIRST in the morning, before anything else happens to him — before medication is managed, before a schedule is read to him, before he is a person who receives care. For a few minutes, he is a person who acts. He initiates.
contraindications: **DRIVING FLAG (Absolute):** Per §5.1, if the chosen anchor involves any equipment, sharp objects, or heat sources, the care team should confirm the father's current capacity. Safety assessments are ongoing, not one-time.

**SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** Elevated suicide risk post-diagnosis and at capability-loss transitions (Draper et al. 2010; Erlangsen et al. 2008). Morning can be a vulnerable time. If the father wakes in acute distress, the morning anchor yields to care. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.
**Situational:** On days when the father wakes confused, agitated, or significantly disoriented, the caregiver gently adjusts. The anchor may be simplified or set aside entirely.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: confusion about the steps of the anchor that was previously easy (a signal that the anchor may need simplification, not that the practice should end), frustration or anger at being unable to complete it, tearfulness on waking that does not settle, refusal to get out of bed that feels different from ordinary tiredness. - What to do: The caregiver meets the father where he is. If the anchor is too complex today, simplify. If the father cannot get up, the anchor can come to him (see Modifications). If the distress is acute and sustained, the crisis response path activates. See 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter version:** One sip of a warm drink someone brings. One breath at the window. One stretch while still in bed.

**Lower-energy version (in bed):** The caregiver opens the window from across the room. The father breathes the air from bed. Or: the caregiver places a warm cup in the father's hands while he is still sitting up in bed.

**"Hard day" version:** The father sits on the edge of the bed. He puts his feet on the floor. He feels the floor. That is the morning anchor for a hard day. Feet on the floor means: I am here. I am starting my day.

**Caregiver-assisted version:** The caregiver prepares materials but does not do the anchor for the father. The caregiver's role is to make initiation possible, not to initiate on his behalf. "Your kettle is ready" or "The window is unlocked." Then step back. If the father needs physical assistance, the caregiver assists with the body mechanics while the father remains the one who is doing the thing.
traditions: - Disability-rights advocacy in younger-onset dementia — Kate Swaffer's concept of prescribed disengagement (Swaffer, What the Hell Happened to My Brain?, 2016) and the DAI disability-rights framework. The counter-practice of preserved agency draws directly on this advocacy. - Zen Buddhism — the monastic tradition of samu (work practice) as a form of practice that honors the body's activity. The morning anchor is closer to samu than to zazen: the practice is in the doing. (Dogen, Tenzo Kyokun / Instructions to the Cook, 13th century.) - Jewish tradition — Modeh Ani, the first words spoken upon waking ("I give thanks..."), recited before any other act. The structural parallel: a person's first conscious gesture of the day is their own, preceding all obligations. (Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 1:1.) - Qigong tradition — morning standing practices (zhan zhuang) that work with the body's vitality upon waking. (Lam Kam Chuen, The Way of Energy, 1991.)

protocols.title: Bedtime Story Voice Recording
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.2) names connection-capsules as practices designed to create durable artifacts of the father-child relationship. Voice recordings of ordinary things — bedtime stories, a song he loves, everyday talk — capture the father's voice as it is, not as a monumental statement. Oliver Sacks documented that procedural and musical memory persist when declarative memory does not (Musicophilia, 2007); a father's reading voice carries prosody, warmth, and rhythm his children imprinted on as infants. Harvey Max Chochinov's Dignity Therapy (2005, 2011), adapted here, asks: what do you want your children to carry forward? The answer, for a young father, may not be wisdom. It may be the sound of his voice reading Goodnight Moon.

The continuing-bonds framework (Klass, Silverman, & Nickman, 1996) established that maintaining an ongoing connection with a person who is changing or gone is healthy, not pathological. A recording made on a good day is not a farewell. It is an ordinary moment preserved — the kind of moment that, years later, may help a child remember not the illness but the father.
This practice requires explicit consent. The father is the author of this artifact, not its subject.
contraindications: **DRIVING FLAG (Absolute):** This practice does not involve driving. However, per §5.1, driving status must be verified with the care team at each stage of the condition. No protocol in this kit assumes driving capacity.

**SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** The younger-onset dementia literature (Draper et al. 2010; Erlangsen et al. 2008) documents elevated suicide risk in the immediate post-diagnosis window and at major capability-loss transitions. If the father is experiencing thoughts of self-harm, or if his caregiver notices acute distress, withdrawal, or hopelessness, this practice yields to crisis response. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**EXISTENTIAL-CONTENT FLAG (Caution):** Recording one's voice for children who may listen to it after cognitive capacity has further changed inherently carries existential weight. If the practice surfaces awareness of decline, despair, or a sense of recording a farewell rather than a bedtime story — stop. The recording is not urgent. Consult the care team if the existential distress is acute. This is the existential-content contraindication from §5.1.
**Situational:** If the father is confused, agitated, or distressed today, do not attempt this. It requires a day when reading aloud feels natural, not forced.
**Relational:** If the father expresses reluctance — even mild reluctance — do not proceed. This practice belongs to him. His "not today" is final for today.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: stumbling over words in a way that frustrates rather than amuses him, visible distress at hearing his own voice falter, tearfulness, flat affect, pulling away from the book, asking "why are we doing this?" in a tone that suggests confusion rather than curiosity. - What to do: Stop the recording gently. Offer water or quiet company. No explanation needed. If the distress is acute — thoughts of self-harm, inability to settle across hours, withdrawal from all engagement — activate the crisis response path. See 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter version (5 minutes):** Read one page or one short poem instead of a full book. A single nursery rhyme is enough.
**Song instead of story:** If reading is difficult today but singing is not, record a song instead. A lullaby. A silly song. A hummed melody. The voice is the point, not the words.
**Dictated version:** If the father cannot read but can speak, the caregiver may hold the book and show pictures while the father talks about what he sees. That conversation is the recording.

**Caregiver-assisted version:** The caregiver opens the book to the first page, places it in the father's hands or on his lap, starts the recording, and sits nearby. If the father pauses and cannot find his place, the caregiver may gently point to where he left off. The caregiver does not read for him unless he asks.
**"Hard day" version:** Do not attempt this practice. It is for good days only.
traditions: - Chochinov Dignity Therapy (adapted) — Harvey Max Chochinov's protocol for helping people with life-limiting conditions create a document of what matters most. Adapted here because the father's cognition fluctuates and the original protocol's interview format may not always be accessible. The adaptation: capture the ordinary rather than the monumental. (Chochinov et al., Journal of Clinical Oncology, 2005; Chochinov, Dignity Therapy: Final Words for Final Days, 2011.) - Continuing-bonds theory — Dennis Klass, Phyllis Silverman, and Steven Nickman challenged the assumption that grief requires "letting go" and showed that maintaining connection with the changed or deceased is healthy across cultures. The voice recording is a continuing-bonds artifact. (Klass, Silverman, & Nickman, Continuing Bonds: New Understandings of Grief, 1996.) - Jewish tzavaah (ethical will) — the tradition of leaving not material goods but values, stories, and blessings for the next generation. A bedtime story recording is a modern tzavaah in miniature — not a formal document but a transmission of presence. (Riemer & Stampfer, So That Your Values Live On: Ethical Wills and How to Prepare Them, 1991.) - Japanese monogatari (narrative tradition) — the practice of transmitting stories across generations as living artifacts. The Genji Monogatari tradition holds that a story told aloud carries the teller's spirit. A father reading Goodnight Moon is a monogatari in its simplest form. (Murasaki Shikibu, The Tale of Genji, c. 1000 CE; Ivan Morris, The World of the Shining Prince, 1964.) - Oliver Sacks on procedural memory — the neurological observation that voice, rhythm, and prosody persist when narrative memory thins. The father's reading voice is held in systems that declarative memory loss does not reach. (Sacks, Musicophilia: Tales of Music and the Brain, 2007.)

protocols.title: One-Sentence Letter
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.2) identifies connection-capsules as low-stakes, consent-dependent practices that create durable artifacts of the father-child relationship. A one-sentence letter is the smallest possible capsule: one sentence per child, written or dictated on a good day, placed in an envelope, and held in trust.

This is not a legacy letter. It is not a farewell. It is not a summary of a life. It is one sentence — "I love you," or "You made me laugh today," or "I am proud of you" — written when the words come easily and the day is clear. Harvey Max Chochinov's Dignity Therapy (2005, 2011) demonstrated that even brief acts of generativity — leaving something behind for someone you love — may help preserve a sense of purpose and meaning. The adaptation here is radical simplicity: one sentence, because one sentence is achievable on a day when a paragraph is not.

The continuing-bonds framework (Klass, Silverman, & Nickman, 1996) holds that ongoing connection with a person who is changing or gone is healthy. A sentence written today may reach a child years from now — not as a relic of loss but as evidence of love that was active and present.
contraindications: **DRIVING FLAG (Absolute):** This practice does not involve driving. However, per §5.1, driving status must be verified with the care team at each stage of the condition. No protocol in this kit assumes driving capacity.

**SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** The younger-onset dementia literature (Draper et al. 2010; Erlangsen et al. 2008) documents elevated suicide risk in the immediate post-diagnosis window and at major capability-loss transitions. If the father is experiencing thoughts of self-harm, or if a caregiver notices acute distress, withdrawal, or hopelessness, this practice yields to crisis response. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**EXISTENTIAL-CONTENT FLAG (Caution):** Creating artifacts for a future in which the father may not be cognitively present inherently carries existential weight. If the practice surfaces awareness of decline, loss of identity, or despair about the future — if the father says anything suggesting the letter feels like a farewell or a final act ("this is the last thing I'll write") — the practice has shifted from generative to anguished. Stop, offer presence, and consult the care team. This is the existential-content contraindication from §5.1.

**CONSENT AND CAPACITY-LOSS (Critical):** The father must give clear consent before each letter. Consent is renewable and withdrawable at any time, including retroactively — if he later asks that a letter not be kept or delivered, it is removed without argument. **If the father's condition progresses to the point where he can no longer meaningfully consent,** the decision about whether to continue delivering previously-written letters rests with the person holding his healthcare power of attorney (POA), or — if no POA is designated — with the family therapist in consultation with the care team. Letters already sealed remain in the mother's trust. No new letters are created without clear consent. This should be discussed with the care team early, ideally before the first letter is written.

**Situational:** If writing or dictating a sentence causes visible distress — if the father becomes tearful, agitated, or withdrawn — stop. The sentence is not urgent. It can happen another day or not at all.
**Relational:** If the father says "I don't know what to say," that is a complete answer. Do not coach. The sentence will come on its own day, or it will not.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: tears that will not settle, frustration at not finding words, agitation, pulling away from the paper, flat affect, asking "what's the point?" in a tone of despair rather than curiosity. - What to do: Put the pen down. Offer water, quiet company, or a change of activity. No explanation needed. If the distress is acute — thoughts of self-harm, inability to settle, withdrawal from all engagement — activate the crisis response path. See 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Dictated version:** The father speaks the sentence. A caregiver writes it down exactly as he said it, in his words. The caregiver reads it back to confirm. The father's voice is the author even if his hand is not the writer.
**Drawn version:** If words are not coming but the father can draw, a small drawing — a heart, a stick figure, a sun — is a sentence in another language. It goes in the envelope the same way.
**Shorter version:** One sentence for one child. Not both. Today's sentence is for this child. Another day's may be for the other.

**Caregiver-assisted version:** The caregiver prepares the paper, envelope, and pen. Sits nearby. If the father asks "What should I say?" the caregiver may respond: "Whatever comes to you" or "What do you want them to know?" — but does not suggest specific words. If the father cannot begin, the caregiver may offer: "Some people start with 'I love you' — but it's your sentence." If the father still cannot begin, that is today's answer. The envelope stays empty and ready for another day.
**"Hard day" version:** Do not attempt. This practice is for good days.
traditions: - Chochinov Dignity Therapy (adapted) — the practice of creating a brief generativity document for loved ones. Adapted here to its smallest form: one sentence rather than a structured interview. (Chochinov et al., Journal of Clinical Oncology, 2005; Chochinov, Dignity Therapy: Final Words for Final Days, 2011.) - Jewish tzavaah (ethical will) — the centuries-old tradition of leaving not material wealth but values, blessings, and love for the next generation. A single sentence of blessing is the elemental form of the tzavaah. The tradition holds that what a parent transmits to a child is not property but presence. (Abrahams, Hebrew Ethical Wills, 1926; Riemer & Stampfer, So That Your Values Live On, 1991.) - Japanese monogatari and ikigai — the narrative tradition of transmitting meaning through story, and the concept of ikigai (a reason for being) — here expressed in the smallest possible form: one sentence that says "you are my reason." (Mogi, The Little Book of Ikigai, 2017; broader monogatari tradition from The Tale of Genji, c. 1000 CE.) - Continuing-bonds theory — the research-based understanding that maintaining connection with a person who is changing or gone is healthy, not a failure to "move on." Each sentence is a continuing bond. (Klass, Silverman, & Nickman, Continuing Bonds, 1996.) - Mexican cartas y recuerdos — the Latin American tradition of written remembrances and letters kept within families across generations. Recuerdos (keepsakes, remembrances) serve as physical anchors of relationship that persist across absence and loss. The tradition is especially strong in Mexican and Central American families where migration, illness, and separation have made written artifacts a primary vehicle for love across distance. (Gutierrez, in Klass et al., 1996; broader cultural practice.)

protocols.title: Shared Book That Lives in Both Houses
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.2) names "a shared object that lives in both houses" as a connection-capsule candidate — a physical artifact that accumulates the relationship over time without requiring any single act to be monumental. A notebook that travels between the father's home and the children's home via visits becomes a living record of the family's ongoing connection: a sentence, a drawing, a sticker, a pressed leaf, a scribble, a nothing. No one is required to add anything on any given day. The book accumulates.

Donald Winnicott's concept of the transitional object (1953, 1971) describes how a child's blanket or stuffed animal bridges the gap between self and other, presence and absence. This notebook is a transitional object for the whole family — a thing that belongs to no single house but to the relationship between houses. It carries the father's mark to the children and the children's marks to the father. It does not require narrative memory to participate in. A pressed leaf does not need to be remembered to be real.

The continuing-bonds framework (Klass, Silverman, & Nickman, 1996) holds that maintaining ongoing connection is healthy. This book is a continuing bond in physical form — not a memorial but a living document, added to by everyone, belonging to everyone.
contraindications: **DRIVING FLAG (Absolute):** This practice does not involve driving. However, per §5.1, driving status must be verified with the care team at each stage of the condition. No protocol in this kit assumes driving capacity.

**SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** The younger-onset dementia literature (Draper et al. 2010; Erlangsen et al. 2008) documents elevated suicide risk in the immediate post-diagnosis window and at major capability-loss transitions. If the father is experiencing thoughts of self-harm, or if a caregiver notices acute distress, withdrawal, or hopelessness, this practice yields to crisis response. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**CONSENT AND CAPACITY-LOSS (Critical):** The father participates by choice. If he no longer wants the book, it stops visiting his house. **If the father's condition progresses to the point where he can no longer meaningfully consent to or understand the book's purpose,** the caregiver and family therapist together decide: does the book continue to travel (with the caregiver making or assisting entries on the father's behalf), or does it stay at the mother's house? Previous entries by the father remain — they are part of the book's honest record. If the father, while still able, expressed a wish about the book's future, that wish is honored. This should be discussed with the care team early.

**Situational:** If the book itself becomes a source of distress — if the father is agitated by seeing entries he does not remember making, or if either child resists the book — pause the practice. The book will wait.

**Relational:** If the supervisor during a visit has concerns about the book (its content, its role in the visit), those concerns take precedence. The book must remain readable by the supervisor at all times.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: distress at seeing entries he does not remember, confusion about whose book it is, agitation when the book is present, refusing to look at it. - In either child: resistance to the book ("I don't want to"), using the book to carry messages between parents, distress at the father's entries (or lack of entries). - What to do: Remove the book from the visit gently. It does not need to be present every time. If the distress is acute, see 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **One-direction version:** The book stays at the father's house. Children add to it during visits. It does not travel. This simplifies logistics and avoids the risk of the book being forgotten or lost in transit.
**Digital companion:** Photos of book pages can be taken (with consent) so both houses have a copy even when the physical book is elsewhere. The physical book remains primary.

**Simpler version:** Instead of a notebook, a single large piece of paper taped to a wall at the father's home, where everyone adds a mark during each visit. When it fills, it goes into a folder and a new sheet goes up.

**Caregiver-assisted version:** The caregiver brings the book to the father, opens it to a blank page, and places a pen nearby. If the father picks it up, the caregiver steps back. If the father does not, the caregiver may say, "The kids added something last time — want to see?" and open to a recent page. If the father engages, the caregiver stays nearby. If not, the book goes away quietly.
**"Hard day" version:** The book stays in its bag. Not every visit needs the book. Its absence is not a loss.
traditions: - Winnicott's transitional object — Donald Winnicott's observation that objects bridging presence and absence (a child's blanket, a parent's scarf) serve a vital psychological function. This book is a transitional object for the whole family. (Winnicott, "Transitional Objects and Transitional Phenomena," International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, 1953; Playing and Reality, 1971.) - Japanese zuihitsu — the literary tradition of the "pillow book" or miscellaneous notebook, epitomized by Sei Shonagon's Makura no Soshi (c. 1000 CE). A zuihitsu contains whatever the writer notices: lists, observations, drawings, impressions, weather. It is not a diary and not a letter. It is a record of being alive and paying attention. This shared book is a family zuihitsu. (Sei Shonagon, The Pillow Book, trans. McKinney, 2006.) - Quaker journaling tradition — the practice of keeping a plain record of inner and outer life, without ornament or performance, as an act of witness. George Fox and early Friends kept journals not for publication but for truth-telling. A family book where "I had cereal for breakfast" sits beside "I miss you" follows this tradition's commitment to the ordinary as sufficient. (Fox, Journal, 1694; Brinton, Quaker Journals, 1972.) - Continuing-bonds theory — the research framework demonstrating that ongoing connection with a person who is changing or gone is healthy. The book is a continuing bond in physical form, growing as the relationship grows. (Klass, Silverman, & Nickman, Continuing Bonds, 1996.) - Mexican ofrendas and recuerdos — the tradition of creating physical spaces and objects that maintain relationship across absence. An ofrenda holds tokens of the beloved; recuerdos (keepsakes) carry presence across distance and time. This book is a portable ofrenda, built together.
