(Brandes, Skulls to the Living, Bread to the Dead, 2006; broader Día de los Muertos tradition.)

protocols.title: Daily Five-Minute Anchor
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.4) identifies a daily anchor practice under five minutes as the first element of the mother's container — the structure that may help her carry grief, caregiving, parenting, and her own life without shattering. The synthesis names three candidate anchors: a Mussar middot check-in, an Ignatian Examen (prospective version), and metta (loving-kindness) directed at herself. Each comes from a different tradition. Each takes five minutes. The mother chooses one door and walks through it.

This is not meditation instruction. It is not therapy. It is five minutes at the start of the day where the mother checks in with herself before the demands begin. The research on caregiver burden (Pearlin et al., 1990; Kaiser & Panegyres, 2006) consistently shows that younger caregivers carry elevated psychological distress and that micro-practices — brief, repeatable, low-barrier interventions — may help sustain daily functioning when larger interventions are inaccessible. Five minutes is a practice a tired mother can actually do.

steps.title: Ask yourself: **Where is my savlanut today?** Not "do I have enough?" Just: where is it? Full, half, running low, empty? No judgment. Just notice.
Ask yourself: **Where is my gevurah today?** Same question. Where is my capacity to hold a boundary? Full, half, running low, empty?
Based on what you notice, name one thing you will protect today. "I will not answer emails after 8 pm." "I will ask for help with pickup." "I will let the dishes wait." One thing. Small.
When the timer sounds, you are done.
steps.body:
---

**Door B: Ignatian Examen — Prospective Version (Jesuit tradition)**

The Ignatian Examen, developed by Ignatius of Loyola (16th c.), is traditionally a retrospective evening practice reviewing the day for *consolations* (moments of life, energy, connection) and *desolations* (moments of drain, anxiety, disconnection). This adaptation is prospective — done in the morning, looking at the day ahead.

The practice:

steps.title: Look at the day ahead. Not the whole week. Just today.
Ask: **Where will consolation likely be today?** A meal with the children, a phone call with a friend, ten minutes of quiet, a walk. Name one thing you can move toward.

Ask: **Where will desolation likely be today?** A difficult phone call, a medical appointment, a moment when the grief hits, the 4 pm wall. Name one thing you can prepare for — not fix, just prepare for.
Hold both: the consolation you will move toward and the desolation you will meet. Neither is the whole day.
When the timer sounds, you are done.
steps.body:
---

**Door C: Metta for Yourself (Buddhist loving-kindness tradition)**

Metta practice, widely taught by authorized Buddhist teachers (Salzberg, 1995; Kornfield, 2008), begins with directing loving-kindness toward yourself — the step caregivers most often skip.

The practice:

steps.title: Sit quietly. Place one hand on your chest or your lap — wherever it feels natural.
Silently repeat these phrases, slowly, with pauses between them:
steps.body:
- "May I be safe."
   - "May I be healthy."
   - "May I be at ease."
   - "May I hold this with grace."

steps.title: You do not need to feel the phrases as true. You are planting them, not harvesting them. Say them whether or not you believe them today.
If a phrase catches — if one of them makes you tearful or resistant or suddenly present — stay with it for a few breaths. That is the one working today.
When the timer sounds, you are done.
steps.body:
---

### Do the practice (every day, same time)

Pick a time. Before the children wake is simplest. But any consistent time works — during a lunch break, after drop-off, in the car before walking into the house. The same time every day builds a rhythm the body recognizes.

Go to your spot. Do your door. Five minutes or less. When you are done, you are done.

contraindications: **MATERNAL SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** If you are experiencing intrusive thoughts of self-harm, inability to care for the children, or persistent dissociation, activate the escalation path before continuing any practice. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`. This practice is not a substitute for professional help.

**SUBSTANCE USE FLAG (Caution):** The anticipatory-grief literature notes elevated alcohol and substance use in young caregivers. If you notice an increase in use patterns, this is a signal the container needs reinforcement — not more demand. Talk to your therapist or a trusted contact. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**Collapsing-baseline week:** If your baseline has been collapsing for several days — sleep severely disrupted, unable to eat, unable to be present with the children, unable to work — return to anchor only. This means: the five-minute practice is the only practice you do. Everything else in the kit pauses. If the collapse continues beyond a few days, activate the escalation path in `00-crisis-response-card.md`.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In yourself: increasing agitation during the practice instead of settling, intrusive thoughts that overwhelm the practice, crying that will not ease and bleeds into the rest of the morning, dissociation (feeling like you are watching yourself from outside), numbness that frightens you. - What to do: Stop the practice gently. Get a glass of water. If you can, name what happened to yourself: "That was hard today." If the distress is acute — thoughts of self-harm, inability to care for the children, persistent dissociation — activate the escalation path. See 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter version (2 minutes):** Door A: ask only "Where is my savlanut today?" — one question, one answer. Door B: name one consolation ahead. Door C: say the phrases once through, slowly.

**Collapsing-baseline version:** Do not do any of the three doors. Instead, your anchor is: sit or stand still for 60 seconds. Breathe. Say to yourself, silently or aloud: "I am still here. That is enough." That is the whole practice.
**Walking version:** If sitting still is impossible today, do the practice while walking — around the block, to the mailbox, pacing the hallway. The questions or phrases come with you.
**Written version:** If speaking internally is hard, write the check-in in a notebook or on your phone. Three lines maximum.
traditions: - Mussar — the Jewish ethical-cultivation tradition, systematized by Rabbi Israel Salanter (19th c.) and recently revitalized by Alan Morinis (Everyday Holiness, 2007) and Ira Stone (A Responsible Life, 2006). The middot of savlanut and gevurah are drawn from the classical Mussar curriculum. The daily check-in practice is adapted from the Mussar cheshbon ha-nefesh (accounting of the soul). - Ignatian Examen — the Jesuit practice of daily discernment developed by Ignatius of Loyola (Spiritual Exercises, 1548). The prospective adaptation is informed by Dennis Linn, Sheila Fabricant Linn, and Matthew Linn (Sleeping with Bread, 1995) and Jim Manney (A Simple, Life-Changing Prayer, 2011). George Aschenbrenner's essay "Consciousness Examen" (1972) established the modern form. - Metta (loving-kindness) meditation — a foundational Buddhist practice taught openly across Theravada and Mahayana traditions. The phrasing here follows Sharon Salzberg (Loving-Kindness: The Revolutionary Art of Happiness, 1995). The practice is offered as a secular-accessible anchor; its Buddhist lineage is honored.

protocols.title: Weekly Grief Window
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.4) grounds this practice in Stroebe and Schut's (1999) dual-process model of coping with bereavement, which proposes that healthy grieving involves oscillation between loss-oriented coping (being with the grief) and restoration-oriented coping (attending to ongoing life). Both are needed. Being stuck in either is unhealthy. For anticipatory grief — grief for a person who is still here but changing — the oscillation is especially important: a mother in continuous grief mode cannot parent effectively, cannot work, cannot rest.

The practical application is scheduled grief time — one hour per week with a specific ritual container. This is not repression. The hour is real. What the structure does is honor the grief by giving it dedicated space while protecting the rest of the week from being entirely consumed. It also protects the children, who are deeply attuned to a parent's emotional state and who should not be asked to absorb a parent in continuous grief mode.

The Jewish mourning tradition offers the most articulated model of structured grief in world practice: aninut (the raw hours), shiva (seven days), shloshim (thirty days), yahrzeit (annual). Each stage has its container. The grief is not denied. It is given a shape. This weekly window draws on that structural wisdom.
contraindications: **MATERNAL SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** If you are experiencing intrusive thoughts of self-harm, inability to care for the children, or persistent dissociation, do not enter the grief window alone. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`. If you want to do the grief window during a difficult period, do it with your therapist present — by phone or in person.

**SUBSTANCE USE FLAG (Caution):** If alcohol or substances have become part of how you cope, the grief window should not include them. If you notice that the grief window has become a drinking hour, that is a signal the container needs professional reinforcement. Talk to your therapist or a trusted contact. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**COMPLICATED-GRIEF-RISK FLAG (Caution):** Complicated grief is clinically distinct from ordinary anticipatory grief — it involves prolonged, unresolved grief that significantly impairs functioning across domains. Signs: grief that does not oscillate (no restoration-oriented days at all for weeks), inability to function in any area of life for an extended period, persistent preoccupation with the loss that excludes all other thought. **If the grief window consistently triggers overwhelming responses that bleed into hours and impair parenting, week after week,** discuss with your therapist whether the grief-window format is right for your current capacity, or whether the therapist-present modification should become the default. Complicated grief is not a failure. It is a signal that professional support should hold this alongside you.
**Collapsing-baseline week:** If your baseline has been collapsing for several days, the grief window may be too much. See the collapsing-baseline modification below.
**When children are present:** Never. This practice is structurally dependent on the children being elsewhere.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In yourself: dissociation during the hour (feeling detached from your body, watching yourself from above), grief that will not ease after the candle is blown out and bleeds into hours, inability to return to parenting after the window closes, intrusive images or thoughts that frighten you, a sense of falling rather than sitting with grief. - What to do: Blow the candle out early. That is always allowed. Get a glass of water. If the distress will not settle, call your therapist or a trusted contact. If you are in acute distress — thoughts of self-harm, inability to function — call 988 or your local crisis line. See 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter version (30 minutes):** Light the candle. Set the timer for 30 minutes. Same practice, half the time. Some weeks 30 minutes is the right container.

**Collapsing-baseline version:** Skip the grief window entirely this week. On a collapsing-baseline week, your only practice is `28-daily-five-minute-anchor.md` in its collapsing-baseline form ("I am still here. That is enough"). The grief window returns when your baseline stabilizes.

**With a therapist present:** If the grief window feels too raw to do alone, schedule it during or immediately before a therapy session. The therapist holds the container. This is not weakness — it is wisdom.

**Walking version:** If sitting in a room with a candle feels claustrophobic, the grief window can be a walk — alone, with no destination, no podcast, no phone call. The walk begins when you leave the door. The walk ends when you return. The container is the walk itself.
**Letter-writing version:** Each week, write one letter to the father. You do not send it. You keep it or you destroy it — your choice. The writing is the practice, not the product.
traditions: - Stroebe and Schut dual-process model — the bereavement research framework proposing that healthy grieving oscillates between loss-oriented and restoration-oriented coping. The grief window is a structured loss-orientation practice; the rest of the week is restoration-orientation. (Stroebe & Schut, "The Dual Process Model of Coping with Bereavement," Death Studies, 1999.) - Jewish mourning structure — the most articulated system of structured grief in world practice. Aninut (raw grief before burial), shiva (seven days of community-held mourning), shloshim (thirty days of gradual return), yahrzeit (annual remembrance). Each stage has a container. The grief is given a shape, not denied. The candle in this practice draws on the ner neshama (soul candle) lit during shiva and on yahrzeit. (Maurice Lamm, The Jewish Way in Death and Mourning, 1969/2000; Anita Diamant, Living a Jewish Life, 1991.) - Psalms of lament — the Hebrew Bible's tradition of speaking grief, anger, and complaint directly, without requiring resolution or piety. Psalms 22, 88, and 130 model a prayer that says "this is unbearable" without pretending otherwise. The grief window gives space for this voice. (Brueggemann, The Message of the Psalms, 1984.)

protocols.title: Weekly Rest Container
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.4) names a weekly rest container as a structural element of the mother's support — not optional self-care but commanded rest, drawn from traditions that understood rest as an obligation rather than a luxury.

Abraham Joshua Heschel wrote that the Sabbath is "a palace in time" — a sanctuary not in space but in the rhythm of the week (The Sabbath, 1951). The principle is older than any single tradition: one day in seven, the work stops. Not because the work is done, but because the person doing the work is more than the work.

For a mother holding two young children, anticipatory grief, court logistics, medical appointments, and her own survival, rest does not happen unless it is structurally protected. "I'll rest when I can" means she never rests. A container — a specific period with specific boundaries — may help because it removes the decision from the moment. The decision is already made. The container holds it.
This is not the grief window (that is 29-weekly-grief-window.md). Rest is rest. Grief is grief. They are different practices for different needs.
contraindications: **MATERNAL SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** If you are experiencing intrusive thoughts of self-harm, inability to care for the children, or persistent dissociation, do not enter extended solitude without first talking to your therapist or a trusted contact. If solitude feels dangerous today, this is important information. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**SUBSTANCE USE FLAG (Caution):** If alcohol or substances have become part of how you cope, the rest container should not include them. If you notice that rest time becomes using time, that is a signal the container needs professional reinforcement. Talk to your therapist. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.
**When no other adult is available for the children:** Do not attempt this. Rest that requires worrying about the children is not rest.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In yourself: inability to stop doing things during the rest period (compulsive cleaning, checking phone, making lists), anxiety that escalates rather than settles during rest, dissociation, intrusive thoughts, a sense that being alone is dangerous. - What to do: If you cannot settle into rest, that is information, not failure. Some weeks the container will feel spacious. Some weeks it will feel unbearable. If the unbearable feeling persists or frightens you, end the container early and call your therapist or a trusted contact. If you are in acute distress, see 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **4-hour version:** The minimum viable container. Four hours without screens, obligations, or caregiving. Even this may feel revolutionary.

**Collapsing-baseline version:** On a collapsing-baseline week, do not attempt a full rest container. Instead, take one hour — not for rest, not for grief, just one hour where you are not responsible for anything. Even if you spend it anxious on the couch. The hour of not-being-responsible is the practice.

**"I can't get another adult" version:** If no other adult is available and the children are old enough to be safe for 30 minutes (8-year-old watching the 5-year-old with clear safety rules, your phone nearby), take 30 minutes behind a closed door. This is not the full container. It is what is possible today.
**Shared rest:** If another adult — a friend, a sister, a mother — is available and wants to rest alongside you (parallel rest, not socializing), that counts. Silence together is a form of rest.
traditions: - Shabbat (Jewish tradition) — the commandment to rest on the seventh day is among the oldest structural rest practices in world civilization. Heschel called Shabbat "a palace in time" — holiness located not in a place but in a rhythm. The exclusions in this practice (no work, no production, no commerce) draw directly on Shabbat's melakhot (categories of prohibited labor), adapted as principles rather than halakhic obligations. (Abraham Joshua Heschel, The Sabbath, 1951; Blu Greenberg, How to Run a Traditional Jewish Household, 1983.) - Christian Sabbath / Lord's Day rest — the tradition of Sunday as a day set apart, drawing on the Decalogue commandment and the early church's shift to the first day. The principle of rest as commanded — not earned, not optional — is shared across Jewish and Christian Sabbath theology. (Wendell Berry, Sabbaths, 1987; Dorothy Bass, Receiving the Day, 2000.) - Tiffany Shlain's Technology Shabbat — the contemporary secular practice of a 24-hour technology fast, one day per week, as described in Shlain's 24/6: The Power of Unplugging One Day a Week (2019). Shlain's family practice provides a modern, accessible model for screen-free rest. The exclusion of screens in this practice draws on her framework.

protocols.title: One Peer Pathway
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.4) names the mother's peer isolation as a double absence: absence from her own peer group (whose concerns do not map onto hers) and absence from older caregiver communities (where her concerns do not map either). Cohen and Wills (1985) established the social-support buffering hypothesis; Holt-Lunstad et al. (2015) showed social isolation carries mortality risk comparable to smoking. For younger caregivers, the isolation is specific and acute: her friends are navigating careers, young-child logistics, and ordinary partnership frictions. Her situation does not fit.

The synthesis is direct: "finding one other person in a similar situation may matter more than finding a large community." One peer who understands without translation is worth more than a twenty-person group where she has to explain her life each time.
This protocol does not ask the mother to join a support group. It asks her to find one person. One pathway. One connection where she does not have to translate.
contraindications: **MATERNAL SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** If you are experiencing intrusive thoughts of self-harm, inability to care for the children, or persistent dissociation, this practice is not the right first step. Activate the escalation path first. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`. Peer connection matters, but crisis response comes first.

**SUBSTANCE USE FLAG (Caution):** If alcohol or substances have become part of how you cope, be aware that peer connection can sometimes include normalization of coping strategies that are not serving you. If a peer relationship increases rather than decreases your substance use, that is a signal to talk to your therapist. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**Collapsing-baseline week:** If your baseline has been collapsing, the energy required to search for and reach out to a peer is too much. Return to `28-daily-five-minute-anchor.md` only. The peer pathway will be here when your baseline stabilizes.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In yourself: the search itself becomes another source of exhaustion and obligation, resentment at having to "work" to find support, despair after unsuccessful attempts, compulsive searching that displaces rest and parenting. - What to do: Pause the search. It is not urgent. The search can resume in a week, a month, whenever energy permits. If the despair is acute, talk to your therapist or see 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Collapsing-baseline version:** Do not search. Instead, tell one person who already knows your situation — your therapist, your mother, a close friend — "I need someone who understands this. Can you help me look?" Delegate the search. Asking for help finding help is itself a form of the practice.

**Online-only version:** If in-person connection feels too exposed or logistically impossible, an online exchange — a forum thread, a private message chain, an email correspondence — is a real connection. It does not need to be face-to-face to count.
**Therapist-facilitated version:** Ask your therapist to make the introduction. Some therapists run informal peer-matching for caregivers. This removes the burden of searching from you entirely.

**"I found someone but it's complicated" version:** If you find a peer but the relationship is draining rather than sustaining — if they are in deeper crisis than you, if the dynamic is one-directional, if it adds burden — you are allowed to step back. One peer means one person who helps. A connection that costs more than it gives is not the right one, and setting it down is not abandonment.
traditions: - Bikkur cholim (Jewish visiting the sick) — the obligation to visit and support those who are ill is a mitzvah (commandment) in Jewish law — the obligation is on the community, not on the sufferer to seek help. This reframes the mother's search: she is not begging for support. She is activating an obligation the community already holds. The Talmud (Shabbat 127a) lists bikkur cholim among the acts "whose fruits a person enjoys in this world while the principal remains intact for the world to come." (Fred Rosner, "The Obligation of Visiting the Sick," in Medicine and Jewish Law, 1990.) - Sangha (Buddhist community of practice) — in Buddhist tradition, the sangha (community of practitioners) is one of the Three Refuges — Buddha, Dharma, Sangha. Taking refuge in community is itself a practice, not a means to practice. One peer is a sangha of two. (Thich Nhat Hanh, The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching, 1998; Sharon Salzberg, A Heart as Wide as the World, 1997.) - Ubuntu (Southern African philosophical tradition) — umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu ("a person is a person through other persons"). The mother's personhood includes her connection to others who see her fully. Isolation is not just loneliness — it is an ontological diminishment. Finding one peer restores something structural. (Desmond Tutu, No Future Without Forgiveness, 1999; John Mbiti, African Religions and Philosophy, 1969.)

protocols.title: Specific Ask Practice
protocols.duration: 15 minutes to prepare (once); 10 seconds to use (each time)
summary: People will say "Let me know how I can help." They mean it. But the mother, exhausted, overwhelmed, and carrying a situation most people cannot imagine, will almost always say "I'm fine" or "I don't know" or "Thanks, I will" — and then never ask. The offer evaporates. The isolation deepens.

The Phase A synthesis (§3.4) identifies a specific-ask practice as a concrete tool: a pre-written list of five actionable asks, kept on a card in her phone case, ready for the moment when "Let me know how I can help" arrives. The preparation happens once, in advance, when she has the bandwidth to think. The use happens in the moment, when she does not.

This practice draws on the Jewish concept of bikkur cholim, which reframes help not as charity from the strong to the weak but as an obligation of the community. When someone says "How can I help?", they are fulfilling an obligation. The mother is not burdening them. She is letting them do what they already want to do. The barrier is not their willingness. The barrier is her ability to name what she needs.

steps.title: Groceries:
steps.body: "Could you pick up [list of 5 items] from [store name]? I can Venmo you."

steps.title: Park time:
steps.body: "Could you take the kids to [park name] for an hour on [day]? They know you and they'll be fine."

steps.title: A meal:
steps.body: "Could you drop off a meal on [day]? Anything works. The kids eat everything except [allergy/preference]."

steps.title: Childcare hours:
steps.body: "Could you watch the kids for 2 hours on [day] so I can [sleep / go to an appointment / sit in my car in silence]?"

steps.title: A phone call:
steps.body:
"Could you call me on [day] evening for 15 minutes? I just need to talk to an adult."

Each ask has: what, when, how long, and any relevant details. The person receiving the ask can say yes or no. Either answer is fine.

### Step 2: Keep the card accessible

Put the card in your phone case, your wallet, your kitchen drawer, or save the note on your phone's home screen. It must be reachable without thinking. When the moment comes — when someone says "What can I do?" — you will not have the bandwidth to think. The card thinks for you.

### Step 3: When the offer comes, name one

When someone says "Let me know how I can help" or "What do you need?" or "I wish I could do something," take out the card (or open the note) and say one of the five asks. Just one. Out loud.

"Actually, yes — could you pick up the kids from school on Thursday? That would help a lot."

That is the whole practice in the moment. The card removes the barrier between the offer and the ask. You do not have to generate the ask in real time. It is already written.

### Step 4: Update the card as needs change

Every few weeks, look at the card. Some asks may no longer be relevant. New needs may have emerged. Update the list. Keep it at five — enough to have options, few enough to remember.

contraindications: **MATERNAL SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** If you are experiencing intrusive thoughts of self-harm, inability to care for the children, or persistent dissociation, your most important ask is not on this card. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`. This practice is for the ordinary asks — the crisis asks have their own protocol.

**SUBSTANCE USE FLAG (Caution):** If alcohol or substances have become part of how you cope, be aware: asks related to "bring me a bottle of wine" or similar should not be on the card. If you notice this impulse, talk to your therapist. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In yourself: guilt after asking (this is normal — it does not mean you did something wrong), shame that you need help (also normal, also not true), compulsive over-asking that strains relationships, inability to ask at all despite having the card. - What to do: Guilt and shame after asking are common in caregivers. They are signals of the isolation, not of wrongdoing. If you cannot ask at all — if the card exists but the words will not come — that is information for your therapist. If the inability is accompanied by other signs of distress, see 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Collapsing-baseline version:** You do not write the card yourself. You ask someone — your therapist, your mother, a close friend — to write it with you or for you. "I need help writing down what I need help with" is itself a legitimate ask.
**Text-message version:** If saying the ask out loud feels impossible, prepare it as a text message template. When someone offers help, send the text instead of speaking.
**Smaller list (3 asks):** If five feels overwhelming, write three. If three feels overwhelming, write one. One ask, written down, ready to deploy, is better than a perfect list that does not exist.

**Reverse version (for friends reading this):** If you are the friend, do not say "Let me know how I can help." Say: "I'm going to the grocery store — what do you need?" or "I'm free Thursday afternoon — I'd like to take the kids to the park." The specific offer is easier to accept than the open-ended one.
traditions: - Bikkur cholim (Jewish visiting the sick) — the reframe that matters most here: in Jewish law, the obligation to help is on the community, not on the sufferer to ask. When the mother names an ask, she is not burdening the asker. She is helping them fulfill an obligation they already hold. The Talmud (Nedarim 40a) teaches that visiting the sick removes one-sixtieth of their suffering — not through grand gestures but through presence and practical help. The specific-ask practice operationalizes bikkur cholim for a modern context. (Fred Rosner, "The Obligation of Visiting the Sick," in Medicine and Jewish Law, 1990; Joseph Telushkin, A Code of Jewish Ethics, Vol. 2, 2009.) - Ubuntu (Southern African philosophical tradition) — umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu — a person is a person through other persons. Asking for help is not weakness. It is the activation of the relational web that makes personhood possible. The mother who asks is not diminished. She is enacting the fundamental structure of human interdependence. (Desmond Tutu, No Future Without Forgiveness, 1999.) - Christian mutual aid (Acts 2:44–45) — the early church practice described in Acts: "All the believers were together and had everything in common. They sold property and possessions to give to anyone who had need." The tradition of concrete, material mutual support — not abstract goodwill but groceries, childcare, meals — has deep roots in Christian community practice. (Christine Pohl, Making Room: Recovering Hospitality as a Christian Tradition, 1999.)

protocols.title: Fast Reset — Sixty Seconds
summary: The Phase A synthesis (§3.4) names a fast reset practice as an essential element of the mother's toolkit — something that takes one minute or less, requires no preparation, no privacy, and no special state of mind, and brings her nervous system back from the edge of overwhelm into a functional range. This is not a spiritual practice. It is nervous-system recalibration.

The physiological basis is well-established. The mammalian dive reflex (Panneton, 2013) triggers a parasympathetic response when cold water contacts the face. Slow exhale-dominant breathing activates the vagus nerve and shifts the autonomic nervous system toward ventral vagal (calm-and-connected) function (Porges, 2011; Dana, 2018). Tactile grounding — hands on a surface, naming what you see — interrupts the dissociative or ruminative loop by redirecting attention to the body's immediate sensory environment (van der Kolk, 2014).
None of this requires belief. It requires sixty seconds.

steps.title: Turn on the cold tap. Or grab a glass of cold water with ice.
Splash cold water on your face — forehead, cheeks, around the eyes. OR: hold your wrists under the cold stream for 10 seconds.

Breathe. The cold triggers the mammalian dive reflex — your heart rate slows, your blood pressure redirects, your parasympathetic nervous system activates. You do not need to understand why it works. It works.
Dry your face. Take one more breath. Done.
steps.body:
Time: 30–60 seconds.

---

**Option B: Three slow breaths with a named exhale**

steps.title: Inhale through your nose for a count of 3.
Exhale through your mouth for a count of 5. As you exhale, say one word silently or aloud. The word is your anchor:
steps.body:
- First exhale: **"Here."**
   - Second exhale: **"Ground."**
   - Third exhale: **"Enough."**

steps.title: Done.
steps.body:
Time: 30 seconds.

The words are not magic. They are anchors. They redirect your attention from the spiral to the present. "Here" = I am in this place. "Ground" = I am in this body. "Enough" = I am sufficient for this moment.

---

**Option C: Hands flat + name three things**

steps.title: Place both hands flat on the nearest surface — table, counter, wall, your own thighs, the steering wheel, the floor.
Press down. Feel the surface under your palms. Feel the temperature, the texture, the solidity.
With your hands pressing, look around and name three things you can see. Out loud if possible, silently if not. "Chair. Window. Cup." Simple nouns. No descriptions needed.
Take one breath. Done.
steps.body:
Time: 30 seconds.

---

### Use your default when the moment comes

The moment: the phone rings with bad news. A child is melting down. The grief hits in the grocery store. The anger flares. The dissociation starts. The overwhelm crests.

Do not think. Do your default. Sixty seconds. Then decide what comes next.

contraindications: **MATERNAL SUICIDALITY FLAG (Caution):** If you are experiencing intrusive thoughts of self-harm, inability to care for the children, or persistent dissociation, this 60-second practice may help in the immediate moment but is not sufficient on its own. After using it, activate the escalation path. Call 988 (US) or your local crisis line. See `00-crisis-response-card.md` and `34-im-at-the-edge-escalation.md`.

**SUBSTANCE USE FLAG (Caution):** This practice is not a substitute for addressing substance use patterns. If you notice that you need the fast reset multiple times a day, every day, that frequency is a signal the container needs reinforcement. Talk to your therapist. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**CARDIAC CAVEAT (Option A only):** The cold-water dive reflex causes bradycardia (heart rate slowing) and peripheral vasoconstriction. This is safe for most people. **If you have a known heart condition, cardiac arrhythmia, or uncontrolled high blood pressure, use Option B or C instead of cold water.** The Kneipp hydrotherapy tradition itself recognizes this contraindication. When in doubt, ask your doctor before using the cold-water option.
**If any flag crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In yourself: the fast reset does not bring you back — you do the 60 seconds and you are still in overwhelm, still dissociated, still unable to function. One reset that does not work is information. Two in a row that do not work is a signal to escalate. - What to do: If the fast reset does not bring you back, move to 34-im-at-the-edge-escalation.md. If children are present and you cannot regulate, follow the escalation protocol's instructions for children-present scenarios. See 00-crisis-response-card.md.
modifications: **Shorter version (15 seconds):** One splash of cold water on the face. Or one slow exhale with "enough." Or hands flat, one thing named. Fifteen seconds is still a reset.

**Collapsing-baseline version:** This IS the collapsing-baseline practice. On days when every other practice is too much, the 60-second reset is the floor. Do it as many times as you need to get through the day. Each one counts.

**With children present:** All three options can be done with children in the room. Option B can even be done while holding a child. Option C can become a game: "Let's both put our hands on the table and name three things we see." The children do not need to know it is a regulation tool. It is a game.
**In the car:** Option B works while driving (eyes open, hands on the wheel, slow exhales with the named words). Option A and C work in a parked car. Never close your eyes while driving.
traditions: This practice draws on physiological and somatic traditions rather than primarily spiritual ones. The traditions honored are:

- Kneipp hydrotherapy — Sebastian Kneipp (1821–1897), a Bavarian priest, developed a system of water-based treatments including brief cold-water applications for physical and emotional regulation. The cold-water face splash in Option A draws on Kneipp's principle that brief cold exposure activates the body's self-regulatory capacities. Modern research on the mammalian dive reflex (Panneton, 2013) provides the physiological mechanism. (Kneipp, My Water Cure, 1891; Mooventhan & Nivethitha, "Scientific evidence-based effects of hydrotherapy on various systems of the body," North American Journal of Medical Sciences, 2014.) - Polyvagal-informed breathwork — Stephen Porges's polyvagal theory (2011) and Deb Dana's clinical translations (2018, 2020) provide the framework for exhale-dominant breathing as a vagal-toning practice. The named exhales ("here," "ground," "enough") add a cognitive anchor to the somatic intervention. (Porges, The Polyvagal Theory, 2011; Dana, The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy, 2018.) - Somatic grounding (trauma-informed) — the hands-flat-on-surface practice draws on somatic experiencing and trauma-informed grounding techniques developed across multiple therapeutic traditions. Bessel van der Kolk's work on body-based trauma processing (The Body Keeps the Score, 2014) and Peter Levine's somatic experiencing (Waking the Tiger, 1997) inform the principle that redirecting attention to the body's immediate sensory contact with the environment interrupts dissociative and ruminative loops.

protocols.title: I'm at the Edge — Escalation Protocol
summary: This is the most important protocol in the mother-alone section of this kit. It exists for the moment when the container is breaking — when overwhelm, grief, rage, dissociation, or despair have crossed from "I'm struggling" to "I cannot do this." The Phase A synthesis (§3.4) names the warning signs directly: "prolonged dissociation, anger that frightens her, inability to cry when grief is clearly present, sleep severely disrupted for multiple nights, thoughts of harm, inability to be present with the children." These are signals that the container needs reinforcement, not that she is failing.

Read this protocol BEFORE you need it. Fill in the contacts NOW, today, while you can think clearly. When the edge arrives, you will not have the bandwidth to search for phone numbers. The numbers will already be here.
The edge is not the end. Reaching out is never weakness. The fact that you are reading this means the container is still holding. You are still here. That is enough to start.
contraindications: **MATERNAL SUICIDALITY FLAG (ACTIVE):** If you are having thoughts of ending your life, call 988 NOW. Do not finish reading this protocol. Call first. Read later. You can also text 988 or text HOME to 741741. Someone is there, right now, who understands caregiver crisis and complicated grief. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.

**SUBSTANCE USE FLAG (ACTIVE):** If you are intoxicated or under the influence and caring for children, call your "I can't care for the kids right now" contact immediately. The children's safety comes first. Your recovery comes next. No judgment. See `00-crisis-response-card.md`.
modifications: **Collapsing-baseline version:** If your baseline has been collapsing, this protocol IS the practice. Everything else in the kit pauses. Your only job is to get through today with the children safe and to reach out for help.
**Non-verbal version:** If you cannot speak, text. Text 988, text 741741 (Crisis Text Line), text your therapist, text your trusted person. "I need help" in a text message is a valid escalation.

**Non-English version:** 988 offers services in English and Spanish. The Crisis Text Line (741741) operates in English. If your primary language is neither, ask your therapist to help you identify a crisis line in your language and add it to the contacts above.
traditions: - Healing-swarm Crisis Response framework — the RECOGNIZE-STOP-GROUND-ASSESS-RESPOND-FOLLOW UP structure that underlies all crisis protocols in this kit. The escalation protocol operationalizes this framework for the mother-alone configuration. - Bikkur cholim (Jewish visiting the sick) — the obligation is on the community, not on the sufferer. When the mother calls for help, she is not burdening anyone. She is activating an obligation the community already holds. The person who comes is fulfilling a commandment, not doing a favor. (Talmud, Shabbat 127a; Nedarim 40a.) - Ubuntu (Southern African philosophical tradition) — umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu — a person is a person through other persons. The mother at the edge is not diminished. She is a person whose web of connection needs to activate. Calling for help is an act of Ubuntu — it trusts that personhood is relational and that the community will hold what the individual cannot hold alone. (Desmond Tutu, No Future Without Forgiveness, 1999.)

protocols.title: Seasonal Family Ritual — If Today Feels Right
protocols.duration: 5–20 minutes (variable; the practice ends when it ends)
summary: Across traditions and centuries, families facing impossible situations have found that one small, repeated, set-apart gesture — done together when conditions allow — may help hold the shape of the family across time. Ritual theory (van Gennep, 1909; Turner, 1969) identifies three stages in rites of passage: separation from ordinary time, a liminal threshold, and return. This practice is a miniature version of that structure: a moment set apart, a small shared act, a return to ordinary life. The act does not require explanation, narrative memory, or a specific cognitive level. It requires only that the people present are willing to be present together for a few minutes. If the father can be there, he is there. If he cannot, the mother and children hold the moment and hold him in mind. Both versions are complete.

steps.title: Gather.
steps.body: Everyone who is present comes to the same place — a table, a patch of ground, a trailhead, a bench. No rush. No announcement beyond: "Let's do our thing."

steps.title: One small shared act.
steps.body:
Choose the version that fits today:

   - **Candle version.** Light the candle together. (The 5-year-old may need help; helping is part of the ritual.) Sit with it for one minute. Each person, if they want to, names one thing they are grateful for. One sentence. "I'm grateful for this park." "I'm grateful for my sister." "I'm grateful we're here." Silence is also allowed. Blow the candle out together or let the youngest blow it out.

   - **Planting version.** Put the seed, bulb, or plant into the soil together. Each person touches the soil. One person waters it. If the family wants to, they name the plant — not ceremonially, just: "This one is the spring one." The plant does not have to survive for the ritual to have worked. The act of planting is the practice.

   - **Walking version.** Walk together to the place. Stand there for a minute. Notice one thing — the light, the sound, the temperature, a bird, a color. One person says what they notice. Others can add theirs or stay quiet. Walk back.

   - **Stone version.** Each person chooses or holds a small stone (or shell, or acorn, or button). Place it in the family's collection — a bowl, a jar, a windowsill shelf. One sentence if it comes: "This one is from April." "This one is the blue one." The collection grows over time without anyone needing to explain it.

steps.title: One breath together.
steps.body: At the end of the small act, everyone pauses. One slow breath. Not a breathing exercise. Just a shared moment of noticing that everyone is in the same place.

steps.title: Return to ordinary time.
steps.body: The ritual is done. No debrief. No "how did that feel?" The family moves to whatever comes next — lunch, a walk, going home, continuing the visit. The transition out is gentle and unmarked.

contraindications: **Absolute:** any configuration that violates the court-ordered arrangement or safety boundaries. If the parents are not currently permitted to be together unsupervised, this practice must happen within an approved supervised setting, or the father-absent version is used instead.

**Relative:** extended all-day family time during acute phases of the father's condition. This practice is short on purpose. It is not a prelude to spending the whole day together. If the family is in an acute phase, the practice should be at its shortest (5 minutes or less) or deferred entirely.

**Caution:** any framing that pressures the father to "be like before" — to remember past rituals in detail, to perform a role he held when his cognition was different, to demonstrate capacity. The practice meets him where he is today.
**If any signal crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: confusion about what is happening that does not settle with gentle guidance; agitation; withdrawal; tearfulness that is building rather than settling. If any appear: the mother gently completes the ritual on behalf of everyone ("Let's take our breath and we're done") and moves on. - In either child: refusal to participate (honored without comment — they can watch), distress, complaints that feel somatic ("my tummy hurts"), sudden energy shift toward agitation. If any appear: shorten to one breath and stop. - In the mother: overwhelming grief surfacing, dissociation, sudden anger. Take a breath. Name it to yourself. The ritual can end here and still be complete. If you cannot settle, the ritual is over for today. That is fine.
modifications: After the small shared act and before the closing breath, the mother says one simple sentence that includes the father: "Daddy is part of our family today too, even though he's not here right now." Or: "We're thinking of Daddy." Or: "This one is for all four of us." The children may add something or stay quiet. The father does not need to be named elaborately. One sentence is enough.
If the family is using the stone version, they may place a stone for the father as well. The 8-year-old may want to choose it. The 5-year-old may want to place it. Either is fine.

If the children ask why Daddy isn't here, the mother uses the language from protocol 13 (*"Why can't Daddy live with us?"*): honest, simple, age-appropriate, not evasive. This is not the moment for a long conversation. "Daddy is at his home today. We're doing our thing and he's part of it."
traditions: This practice draws on structural insights from multiple traditions without requiring the family to adopt any one:

- Ritual theory — van Gennep's three-stage rite of passage (separation / liminal / incorporation) provides the structure: a moment set apart from ordinary time, a threshold act, a return. Turner's communitas names the quality of undifferentiated closeness that sometimes appears in threshold moments. Catherine Bell's practice-theoretic framing: the ritualizing is what makes it ritual, not any external authority. (References: van Gennep, Rites of Passage, 1909; Turner, The Ritual Process, 1969; Bell, Ritual Theory, Ritual Practice, 1992.) - Jewish tradition — the shehecheyanu blessing ("who has kept us, sustained us, and brought us to this moment") as a frame for gratitude at the seasonal turn. The candle-lighting draws on Shabbat and Hanukkah practice without replicating either. (Reference: Lamm, The Jewish Way in Death and Mourning, 1969/2000.) - Christian tradition — the Ignatian Examen's "consolations" practice (noticing where life was given today) as a frame for the one-sentence gratitude. (Reference: Fleming, What Is Ignatian Spirituality?, 2008.) - Japanese tea ceremony — ichi-go ichi-e ("one time, one meeting"), the principle that every encounter is unrepeatable and deserving of complete attention. The "if today feels right, today is the day" framing draws directly on this principle. (Reference: Okakura, The Book of Tea, 1906.) - Secular and humanist approaches — Sasha Sagan's work on secular ritual design within family life; Viktor Frankl's logotherapy as meaning-making without theism. (References: Sagan, For Small Creatures Such as We, 2019; Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946/2006.) - Indigenous philosophical orientation — Robin Wall Kimmerer's writing on reciprocity with the living world informs the planting version. The practice does not enact any closed ceremony; it draws on the openly published philosophical orientation of kinship with all beings. (Reference: Kimmerer, Braiding Sweetgrass, 2013.)

None of these traditions is privileged. The family may draw on whichever resonates, or none at all. The ritual is offered as a universal human form: people who love each other, pausing together, noticing they are here.

protocols.title: Milestone Marker — This Shape, This Day
protocols.duration: 2–10 minutes (the marker is a moment, not an event)
summary: Milestones come whether or not the family is ready for them. Birthdays, first days of school, holidays, the turn of a new year. In families facing progressive cognitive change, milestones can carry a specific kind of pain: the distance between how this day was marked before and how it must be marked now. The impulse is either to perform the old version (exhausting, often impossible) or to skip the milestone entirely (a different kind of loss).

This practice offers a third path: mark the milestone in whatever shape the family takes today. The Japanese principle of ichi-go ichi-e ("one time, one meeting") holds that every encounter is unique, unrepeatable, and complete in itself. Continuing-bonds theory (Klass, Silverman & Nickman, 1996) holds that relationship persists across change and absence — not as denial of loss, but as honest recognition that love does not require the loved one to be unchanged. Together, these frames may help a family mark a milestone without measuring it against what used to be. This birthday, this first day of school, this holiday — in the shape of the family as it is right now — is whole.

steps.title: Name the day.
steps.body: The mother says it simply: "Today is [name]'s birthday." "Today is the first day of school." "Today is [holiday]." One sentence. Not a speech. The naming is itself the separation from ordinary time.

steps.title: One marker.
steps.body:
Choose the version that fits today:

   - **Photograph version.** Everyone present stands or sits together. One photograph. No posing requirements. No "everyone smile." The photograph is of the family in whatever shape they take today. If someone is crying, that is in the photograph. If someone is laughing, that is in the photograph. The photograph is honest.

   - **Shared-book version.** Open the shared book (protocol 27) to the next blank page. Write the date and one sentence. The sentence can come from anyone: "Mia turned 9 today." "First day of second grade." "We had pancakes." The 5-year-old can draw something instead of writing. The father can write his name, or make a mark, or the mother can write for him. One page. Close the book.

   - **Special food version.** Make or eat one special thing together. A pancake with a candle. A cupcake split four ways (or three ways, or two ways). A bowl of the fruit someone loves. The food does not have to be elaborate. Eating together is the marker.

   - **Song version.** Sing one song. "Happy Birthday" counts. A lullaby counts. A song the family used to sing in the car counts. Humming counts. The father humming one note while the children sing the words counts. If nobody can sing today, someone can play the song on a phone and everyone listens for one minute.

   - **Spoken-sentence version.** The mother says one sentence that marks the day: "We are here on [name]'s birthday, and we love [name]." Or: "First day of school. We're proud of you." Or simply: "Today matters." The children can add a sentence or not.

steps.title: One breath.
steps.body: The same shared breath from other practices. A pause. Present.

steps.title: Continue the day.
steps.body: The marker is done. The day continues. The milestone has been honored. No debrief.

contraindications: **Absolute:** any configuration that violates the court-ordered arrangement or safety boundaries. If supervised visitation is in effect, this practice must occur within the structure of an approved visit or an approved family-together occasion. If there is any doubt, check with the visit supervisor or the family's legal representative first. If the milestone falls on a day when the family cannot legally or safely be together, the father-absent version is used.

**Relative:** high-stakes milestone events where performance pressure is inescapable — large family gatherings, formal celebrations with extended family present, events where multiple people have expectations about how the father should appear or behave. This practice is for the private family moment, not the public performance.

**Caution:** anything that frames the milestone as a comparison ("last year, Daddy could..."). The marker honors today. If a child spontaneously mentions a comparison, that is their processing and should be received gently (see protocol 14, *How Was Your Heart Today?*), but the adults do not introduce the comparison.
**If any signal crosses into crisis territory, see `00-crisis-response-card.md`.**
signals: - In the father: distress at the milestone itself (awareness of what has changed, grief at the passage of time, confusion about the occasion). If he is distressed: the mother gently completes the marker without requiring his participation. He can simply be in the room. His presence is his contribution. - In either child: sadness (expected and appropriate — do not fix it), frustration that the day is "not like before" (receive the feeling; do not argue with it), acting out. If distress escalates: the marker is complete as-is. Move on gently. - In the mother: grief that ambushes. Milestones are grief-surge points (Meuser & Marwit, 2001). If grief rises: let it be present. The children can see that milestones can be sad and still marked. If you cannot continue, the marker is done. You can return to it privately later if you want to.
traditions: This practice draws on structural insights from multiple traditions:

- Japanese ichi-go ichi-e — "one time, one meeting." The principle that every encounter is unique, irreplaceable, and deserving of full presence. Applied here: this milestone, in this shape, with these people present, is itself complete. It is not a diminished version of a previous milestone. It is the only version of this one. (Reference: attributed to Sen no Rikyu, 16th c., developed by Ii Naosuke; Okakura, The Book of Tea, 1906.) - Continuing-bonds theory — Klass, Silverman, and Nickman's research (1996) demonstrating that maintaining connection with the changed or absent person is healthy grief, not denial. The father-absent versions of this practice draw directly on this finding: including the father in the milestone by naming him is continuing the bond, not pretending he is unchanged. (Reference: Klass, Silverman & Nickman, Continuing Bonds: New Understandings of Grief, 1996.) - Mexican Dia de los Muertos and continuing relationship — the cultural tradition of marking milestones and occasions with those who are absent, through ofrenda, food, and naming. The structural insight: absence does not end relationship; it changes the form relationship takes. - Jewish yahrzeit and annual marking — the tradition of returning to specific dates to remember and honor. Milestone-marking across the calendar year echoes this rhythm of return. (Reference: Lamm, The Jewish Way in Death and Mourning, 1969/2000.) - Anticipatory mourning research — Rando's (2000) and Boss's (1999) empirical finding that grief surges at transitions and milestones, and that structured containers at those points are protective. The smallness of this practice is deliberate: it provides the container without adding burden. (References: Rando, Clinical Dimensions of Anticipatory Mourning, 2000; Boss, Ambiguous Loss, 1999.) - Secular and humanist approaches — Viktor Frankl's orientation toward meaning in suffering; Sasha Sagan's secular family rituals. A milestone does not require religious framing to be sacred. It is sacred because the family cares about it. (References: Frankl, Man's Search for Meaning, 1946/2006; Sagan, For Small Creatures Such as We, 2019.)
None of these traditions is privileged. The family draws on whichever resonates, or none. The milestone is marked because it matters to someone in the family. That is enough.
